Just a heads up .....

we have a wise card that we transfer money to from our bank account in Aus.

My card was hacked and all the money that was on it was taken.

Their security is pathetic and help to sort this out has been non existent because it is the weekend.

There was and are so many inconsistencies with how They are handling this.

We have to find an alternative.....

5\. rinse and repeat next week

USD
#3 .....You can spend what you want and need to if you have funds on your card
#4 .....You are able to withdraw $350.00 cash free per month - no charges and then
a small fee for more cash withdrawals after that

### caroline — 2022-04-17T19:35:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-312238>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=312237)
>
> Good to know, and much better than I had initially thought.
> Thank you for your clarification.
>
> Now since the Wise card was hacked, how can you get $US from your AU account that is in $AU.
>
> ............

well .... we should be able to use our bank debit card ...but yesterday we went to an ATM with our debit card but it wouldn't work.

We recently had issues because of the sms they send ....and we can't receive -because our Aus sim doesn't work in ES.

I thought I straightened it all out yesterday morning before we went to an ATM - obviously not. I need to make another phone call.

We are so lucky that our host has a land line and lets us borrow her phone to call OZ.

Our host has invited us to live with her. She has a huge, grand home and her family and friends are gorgeous and treat us like gold ....very tempting!

Meeting all these wonderful people has been the highlife of this adventure.

Everyday Salvadorians step up to help us out of some dilemma .......wether it is the grocery store or a department store.

The other day we were buying a coverlet for our bed. We picked out the one we wanted but the saleslady was going a hundred miles a minute in Spanish ....we had no clue and no hope of using google translator. Another shopper came over to us and explained that the saleslady was telling us we would get free sheets with the coverlet!

I asked this gentleman if he would follow us down to the bed department! and then on to washing machines!

You want to keep ES friendly.
Check if it is ok for you to have washing machines.
When speaking with some Indian Indians in USA.
Back home they hire lots of people to do housework.
It was the same in Brazil.
Old habits die hard.
Across the street from me, Indian, over the week, 2, 3 services on the slow week.
....

### Kari Dunlop — 2022-05-10T20:23:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-312883>

Very wise words! I throw a little surrendering into the mix as well. I've found that, for me, things can shift faster (and often in a better direction) when I let go of the grasping (which is usually always coming from my fear) and allow some space for things to move. Get out of my own way.

Reminds me of one of my favourite quotes, "Let go or be dragged' -- Zen proverb.

### caroline — 2022-05-28T20:39:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-313213>

We are settling into our new home in Atami. Everything is a big mess as workers transfer the electrical box and wiring to the other side of the house.
Having an electrical box beside your bed and your head is not ideal.

Shopping for familiar groceries is a challenge.....

Driving in and out of San Salvador is a huge challenge if you go at peak periods.
We got caught a couple of times coming home in the dark - that is a big NO.
The last time it was in the rain - never again.

We are loving having a swimming pool - who knew???
It is great having a swim first thing in the morning and it is fantastic at nite time - before bed.

Tony said something yesterday that made my day ...."I love this house and this country!"
Tony is so energized and this move will add years to our life.

Tony depended on me and trusted my judgement to get this right - more or less. It is a no brainer.
I adore this guy who believes in us 150%

Has this been easy? .....heck no - but we are loving every second of this challenge!

I hear we have dodgy neighbors

### caroline — 2022-06-04T22:37:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-313348>

A retrospective .....
Today is Tonys birthday ....we have a two story rancho - so we sat and watched the ocean and talked. We call this our penthouse.

If someone has said to us one year ago that we would be living in El Salvador with a beach house and a view of the ocean We would hav said no way - not going to happen.

We loved living in Australia and in our little fishing village on the ocean. ......until we understood that nazi germany had arrived on our doorstep.

I am still incensed when I remember the day that we were told that we can't leave our home without our ID.

It was about a year ago when some beach friends invited us up to their apartment/home on the headland overlooking the ocean.

I will never forget how We were in awe of the view from the moment we entered their front door.

I just kept thinking - I want THIS ...but how do I ever afford THIS. I wasn't envious - just in awe that we could live like this someday ....

I kept telling Tony - one day we will live at the ocean. I was very sure about this. I didn't know how or when....

Property in this area in Oz is worth millions especially during covid when it was really discovered as people looked for beach property in Australia.

I thought that once bitcoin starts moving up to where it should be - we will be able to afford something like this....if this is what we want.

Well ....here we are - one year later. We pinch ourselves every single day when we look around ourselves and can't believe we are here and this is ours.

I have never stopped dreaming my whole life. My mother aways said I wanted too much .....but I say - Heck no ....never stopped dreaming - it doesn't cost anything and it is so good for the soul.

It isn't about wanting an expensive car or a house with all the modicums or designer clothes.....I have had my share.

Tony and I wanted to live at the beach and be able to walk to the ocean and live an outdoor life. We never in our wildest dreams thought it would happen - but yet here we are!

I don't really know how we had the guts to sell everything and leave OZ - but we did!

When I look back at this last year - I guess we just took everyday as it came and moved forward little by little.
So many decisions to make and of course family telling Tony that we were out of our minds - in very colorful language and extreme detail.

We have absolutely no regrets - we are both thrilled to be here ....except for those dodgy neighbors we here about .......

### caroline — 2022-06-04T22:51:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-313349>

We are going out for mojitos tonite to celebrate our life and love and incredible good fortune...

We always hear that life is only what you make of it .......

Every one has trials and tribulations - we have had our share....

We have no real idea how we ended up here ......but we will make the most of it.

We both seem to have the ability to pick ourselves up and move forward - especially Tony. He never dwells on the negatives - he just moves forward and gets on with his life.

I am so grateful that he found me ...but I was ready to be found!

Good luck to everyone on this wild ride we call our life....Take no prisoners!

### caroline — 2022-06-08T16:44:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-313448>

We had to go into San Salvador yesterday to get our visa extended. No problem at all.

We did some shopping before we came home. We saw some nice bottles of Malbec so we thought we would get some in case we have random dodgy neighbors drop in for a visit.

The drive home in the dark and rain was a horror - so when we got home we drank the malbec

### Sandra — 2022-06-11T00:44:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-313518>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=313512)
>
> Hi Sandra - so lovely to hear from you. How is your family?
> How is Nick and his family?
> We have wondered about you guys often.....

I have thought of you often too and am so proud of what you are doing.

We are all as well as can be I guess. My family and Nick's family are the only ones in our office of 25 people who have not accepted the brainwashing. Can't really believe what we are seeing and hoping people start to wake up soon. It was tricky with teenage kids in the beginning. One of ours was on our side (she's a bit of a rule-breaker anyway so for once it worked in our favour) and the other has now come around to see through what's going on. It so hard when all their friends are vaxxed and the school is pushing the 'health advice' too. I've been learning to trade crypto currency for a little while now, so very happy to see Jack is also a fan. It's amazing how many people I've met in that space who are all on our side too - they all think outside the square. It's been great to have that support as well, considering only one of our family friends think like us as well. I seriously can't believe it. But some are starting to come around thank goodness - others still won't entertain the conversation at all.

Anyway, the weather is cooling down in Brisbane now and it looks like we may actually experience a winter for a month or so, rather than just a week or so!

I look forward to following your adventures a little more closely from now on and if there's anything you need from here, please let me know.

### caroline — 2022-06-17T22:01:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-313646>

I have been through multiple property renovations in my adult life .....amazing how you can get carried away.

The thing is - with a personal renovation - you have to keep moving forward.

We have to keep reading and re reading and learning to keep moving forward to reap multiple benefits.

We have to reassess our thinking often and be willing to change when we get off track.

There is no downside ....as long as we keep learning with an open mind and heart.

A house renovation is a money pit!!!!

We now have to remind ourselves that we can't expect perfection and we can't do it all.

We now say to ourselves daily ....this is a beach house!

There are so many things that are wonky here ....and the workmanship is a complete joke.

It makes us laugh when we look at all the doors that are hung upside down - and the list goes on.

This will all keep Tony busy for quite some time - he loves it - most days.

But we love this beach house mostly just as it is.

Everyday is a learning curve ....just as it should be.

Keep active, keep engaged, enjoy everyday.........

Live, laugh, love

.

It makes us laugh when we look at all the doors that are hung upside down - and the list goes on.
\============

House has a two or even three shapes, that may affect the workings of installed doors.
It all depends on the temperature the house is in when the doors are installed.
1\. Summer hot days, house open
2\. Winter cold days
3\. Summer hot days, house fully airconditioned.

In my house (NJ USA), I have replaced all doors (with their frames) under condition #1. All works as it should.
Come condition #2 or #3, some doors are tight, and some are hard to close.
Some day will have to do additional adjustments.

.......

### caroline — 2022-06-18T02:54:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-313648>

One big thing we have learned recently .....

Just because a person speaks English relatively well does not mean that they are understanding us and our intended meaning.

There are so many nuances in our language and we are discovering that many things have been misunderstood and misconstrued.

One friend speaks English very well and, in fact, lived in Miami for years.

When we are discussing something she will pay attention and we think she understands and then she will say "Please repeat - I don't understand"

The point is .....many people won't say and we assume that they do understand when they totally don't.

This has caused misunderstandings that we weren't aware of at the time - on important issues.

''''''''''''''''''''''''''''''''''''''''''''''''''
I have a girlfriend now.
She is Polish and speaks polish.
Her polish is 20 years younger than mine. Or even 50 years younger. Since I have very little contact with people in Poland, she constantly talks with her friends in Poland.
Relatively often we need clarifications about what one of us meant saying.
,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,

### 5G Canary — 2022-06-18T16:30:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-313656>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=313648)
>
> "Please repeat - I don't understand"

What a great observation... I just had this conversation with my kids. There seems to be a big communication uptick with all this social media and the way people communicate now. The problem is most people don’t listen to **_understand_**, they listen just enough to **_reply_**. Sounds like you found a keeper! Congratulations on your new home and adventures!

### caroline — 2022-06-18T18:29:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-313659>

> [5G Canary said:](/goto/post?id=313656)
>
> What a great observation... I just had this conversation with my kids. There seems to be a big communication uptick with all this social media and the way people communicate now. The problem is most people don’t listen to **_understand_**, they listen just enough to **_reply_**. Sounds like you found a keeper! Congratulations on your new home and adventures!

we have absolutely noticed that too. People are only half listening and want to move on. People seem to be quite hyper and bored - and really not interested anyway.
When you throw in a language issue - you can have big misunderstandings and assumptions that aren't correct..

That is one thing that Jack has taught us long ago .....

When you have a conversation with someone .......listen - really listen. Most people only pay lip service and want to do the talking and tell you about themselves.

so many misunderstandings can occur ..........

### caroline — 2022-06-20T21:16:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-313695>

Tony and I were lucky enough to be invited to Tom and Nancy's home yesterday.

We are both totally in awe of what they have accomplished so far - and what they intend to do.

They have undertaken a huge project - I can't imagine the mental and physical stamina this project is taking ...but it is going to be truly spectacular!

I need to qualify this though ......Their home won't be beautiful in a traditional sense. There is nothing traditional about this home - it is more about how they are enhancing the old original cassa and the property.

This project of theirs is the ultimate fitness plan . both mentally and physically.

### caroline — 2022-06-23T01:59:16+0300

Everything looks fantastic - so lush and green.

### caroline — 2022-08-06T22:14:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-314895>

update from El Salvador ......

We are settling in more and more and enjoying the sunrises and all the sunshine.

It is very humid and I struggle with that more than Tony ....but of course he can just peel off his shirt!

Tony is busy plastering and painting and all the steps in between. He is so frustrated with the extremely poor workmanship in this house.
It is fantastic that Tony knows how to do most all this stuff - but his language leaves a little to be desired at times

We are really thriving here- especially Tony. I am a work in progress. We have a working kitchen now so that is my happy place.

I have read that when renovating your home- do the master suite first and that is absolutely correct. We now have a lovely comfortable sanctuary at the end of the day - relief from all the frustrations.

I seem to have developed a red meat allergy - pretty interesting for someone who eats mostly carnivore with some veg.

Tony is growing gorgeous roses! and I am the lucky recipient

### JanSz — 2022-08-06T23:00:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-314896>

You may have an Air Conditioning system.
The electric power from the grid may be interrupted.
It is good idea to have good size electric generator handy.
That generator should run on Natural Gas or diesel.
You should have a tank or tanks with enough fuel for at least a month or more.

..

### JanSz — 2022-08-06T23:05:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-314897>

I seem to have developed a red meat allergy - pretty interesting for someone who eats mostly carnivore with some veg.
\===============================

I do not know about you or (likely) many other peoples
including
[@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) followers
but
my life is changing mightily (for the better) since I learned that
human race
it hugely malnourished in potassium for the last 20 or many more millennia.
(And doctors (MD's are clueless about it), except for pocketing $$ and giving wrong advice, to make sure that this deficiency continues).

\=====================================

You are talking red meat (while in El Salvator).
That reminds me of red meat in Brazil in 1979.

That is basically venison. Meat from wild animal. No trace of any fat.
Practically there is no way to eat that meat unless it is grinded.

You are likely would be better off getting pork or seafood.

.............

### caroline — 2022-09-30T19:27:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-316190>

An update from El Salvador........

The AUD$$ is falling against the USD .............I think we should be holding cash in our hot little hands??? - as much as possible.

It is not good to be buying goods and services priced in USD and paying with AUD.

Am I thinking clearly???

We need to adapt our lifestyle to suit the circumstances of our existence here in El Salvador.

We are shopping at the local markets and eating what is seasonal and available - like the Salvadorans do.

Of course that is a no brainer - but sometimes I am a slow learner

I can't really do things/make things like I used to do in Australia - Those things are considered speciality items here and cost a small fortune - especially in AUD.
A 500gr jar of Tahini cost $17.50 USD .......$27.19 AUD. It can stay on the shelf!

We are going native .......

We have discovered watermelon! We have never liked watermelon - too sloppy and all the seeds and just wasn't worth the effort.
Brandon brought some over when they came to visit and they were so delicious.

At a street stall in La Libertad - we bought a watermelon. very large papaya, carrots, potatoes, tomatoes and radishes for $8.00. we probably payed more than a local.
we have to learn to haggle I guess.

We are taking Spanish lessons .........not a piece of cake!

We are struggling to find good tradespeople to do the things that Tony can't.

Some tradesmen do a terrible and incompetent job - but that is a world wide problem.

We are newbies here and have to learn the ropes.

When you know better - you do better.

### JanSz — 2022-09-30T20:03:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-316196>

What is our stance on watermelon? How much of it we are allowed to eat?
I love it (when cold). Mostly no seeds. But seeds really do not bother me.
There is a ton of it at Costco. 7 US$ a piece now, previously was cheaper.

### caroline — 2022-09-30T20:48:55+0300

I figure - in the tropics - we can have watermelon and papaya and mangoes in season?

### caroline — 2022-09-30T21:22:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-316200>

We are going to have to add beans and potatoes and maize to our diet here to maintain some kind of a budget.

When in Rome - do as the Romans do.....

To us - the hard working salvadorans are very fit and healthy. They walk or bike most places they are going.

I am going to put Salvadorans into three classes here - for the sake of this discussion.

The bottom rung are the Salvadorans who work really hard physically cleaning and gardening and general laborers etc. - all those jobs no one else wants to do.

Australians are particularly bad for this - they would sooner take a hand out from the government than pick fruit etc. Farmers have to bring in laborers from outside the country to pick their crops or plow them under.
Picking fruit is a great job. Yes it is hard work but you are outdoors and can keep fit and healthy and it pays good. I picked a lot of fruit in my day to earn pocket money.

The next level of Salvadorians are ones that think they are middle class so they shouldn't have to do all the menial tasks.
They want to have money like all the rich Americans that they see but they don't know how to apply themselves and they sure don't know how to do a competent job worthy of a good days pay.

I suppose this is a world wide issue.

The wealthier Salvadorans , it seems to me, want to keep the Salvadorans on the bottom rung and be at their beck and call for cheap labor.

We were told to be careful and not pay them too much ....or they will get the wrong idea........

from our experience - these Salvadorans who do all the hard yacka - as Tony would say are the ones keeping this country ticking along.

The young man who is helping us get this property in shape works so hard - and he can do many things.
Right now he is helping Tony fix a leak in the pool that the water/pool expert caused by being sloppy and careless.

He and his wife are so kind and generous and have told us to call them anytime we need help - no matter what it is and no matter what time it is - day or night.

Their children sometimes come to work with them and help out.

They take pride in their work and they all work together. If we need extra help for a bigger job that is never a problem.

We admire their work ethic and their generous spirit. They always have a big smile and a greeting.

We have met lots and lots of wonderful Salvadorans who have been very kind and generous and gone out of their way to help us.

We have also met our share of scumbags......

Same problems - different country.

### Lahelada — 2022-10-02T18:09:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-316238>

"A 500gr jar of Tahini cost $17.50 USD .......$27.19 AUD."

"At a street stall in La Libertad - we bought a watermelon. very large papaya, carrots, potatoes, tomatoes and radishes for $8.00. we probably payed more than a local."

Good to hear your update. It is a learning process. I don't think you did badly though. Your bill for all that does not seem so excessive, depending on the amounts you bought. The Sandia alone would probably account for $3.00, the papaya for 1.50 to 2$. The water shortages everywhere have made all produce more expensive than before. Here is a consumer defence site that checks prices every week.
( Click "Ver sondeo" against the latest date)
[https://www.defensoria.gob.sv/servicios/sondeos-de-precios/](https://www.defensoria.gob.sv/servicios/sondeos-de-precios/)

Re Tahini - if you feel like making it yourself,i sesame is called ajonjoli and is not that expensive, as it is locally grown in EV, something like 1USD for 100g. Homemade tastes probably a bit more bitter as you cannot dehull it, but maybe you like.

Regarding learning Spanish it takes time. You can do it!

### Marko Pollo — 2022-10-02T19:16:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-316249>

Si, se puede! I like to study and learn words about subjects that interest me. Vacas, corderos, pollos, y mas. I think it is good to have some simple sentences ready that you might use. El sol no es mi enimigo. No tengo miedo del sol. Me encanta el sol. You might be able to find fresh pitaya. It is similar or the as dragon fruit. Our fav but don't eat to many. There are lots of other fruitas I would encourage you to try. Maracuya, lulo, granadilla, uchuva, fejoa, cherimoya,
platano, banano. Buena suerte.

yes we do know some simple things - we seem to manage if we throw in a lot of hand signals!!

They seem to understand each other perfectly and it is very entertaining.

As long as I keep making cookies - everyone is happy!

I am thinking of my 5 - 7 years living expenses.
Until recently it was acceptable to look up past 7 years expenses, and that was my goal.
Now with all this inflation constantly increasing, that may not be adequate.
\----
In USA for retirement accounts there is
RMD **Required Minimum Distribution**, for 70.5 yo or older.
That distribution must be done with cash.
It is super important to have that amount of cash in the account before or on December 31.
GBTC was much higher in the past Dec 31.
Had I waited that would have been unnecessary los.
To get rid of cash that I know is not really mine, I prepaid the federal taxes on it.

.......................
Whatever is left over goes toward GBTC.

### Marko Pollo — 2022-10-09T21:13:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-316438>

> [ND Hauf said:](/goto/post?id=316391)
>
> I’ve lived a lot of places and uprooted my girls many times…left friends and family behind to only make new ones along the journey…..I often miss old familiar places and it usually hits when I’m least expecting it….here’s to you and your new home tonight……cheers.

Google the 5 stages of culture shock. If you know what is coming, it might be easier to deal with. Almost all people suffer similar reactions. It's not a short process. Convincing yourself you are immune can be a mistake. We would be having all these confusing and frustrating experiences if the world was normal and you were just relocating. The AFUeduppedness of the world now adds exponential uncertainty to the equation. Good luck and may you focus on your dreams until your new life becomes the life of your dreams.

### Inger — 2022-10-13T21:14:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-316581>

Crazy!
Pension should be solid and not something one can take away, if you have been working in that country and earned it. That is how it works in Europe at least. You earn pension depending on how many years you have worked in that country, and you get it even if you move abroad.
Like my dad has worked only a few years in Norway, but he get pension from there every month and he lives in Finland now.

But I am not really surprised they take it away. The world has gone so weird and they can do anything, kinda. If a country goes bankrupt or there is war, the government can take everything from you, your house and land etc.
So that can happen. But our skills, intelligence, joy, peace, love, sun, nature... etc. they cant take that
Life is becoming more of an adventure every day...

### Lahelada — 2022-10-15T02:29:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-316630>

From what I can see two things may be behind that request.
1\. A quest to see whether back taxes may be due and a drains up as to whether pension entitlement really exists.
2\. If a lot of money were to have been transferred to ES it may have triggered a red flag and they may be required to look into whether the sources or destination of that money were legit.

I think you should find out more as to why they are asking. If you do not already know that is. Refusing the info may or may not be in your interest depending on the reason for the enquiry.

### caroline — 2022-10-15T17:22:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-316647>

My pension would not be re instated. They say I didn't reside or work in OZ long enuf - only 18 years.
Strange that I was even able to apply for and get a pension.....

Our money is still in OZ........This is a huge concern to us now.

We are not allowed to have a bank account in El Salvador unless we are granted residency ......which is looking iffy at the moment.

We could have transferred a significant amount of money to our wise card ....but that was hacked months ago - and our card was cancelled for security concerns.
Our money was eventually reinstated but I had to harass them for months.

So now - they are NOT sending wise cards to El Salvador yet ......... so we can't use the money on the card. They tell me I can transfer it back to the bank it came from - but that doesn't make any sense at this point either.

This sounds like a friggin soap opera.......

My strategy at the moment is to drag my feet on compliance.......

I really don't even know how to get 10 years of bank statements. There are multiple banks involved and multiple names.
Tony had his own bank accounts for years and I and my DH did as well.

### JanSz — 2022-10-15T21:53:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-316664>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=316650)
>
> we did have to apply for an exit visa to leave Australia....so I think that is pretty official.
>
> We would not go back to Australia........altho we did love living there for many years.

I am assuming that you still have Australian citizenship and AU passport.
Why did you need AU visa to leave AU?

When I was leaving (officially) Communist Poland
the trick was to get Polish passport.
After I got that pasport, I went to USA ambassy and (at that time) it was easy to get visa to enter USA. Business, vacation, or permanent stay in USA.
Easy to immigrate into USA or go for vacation.
Later, when Poland started giving passports to anybody who asked for it, USA got tough to get into (officially).

Some people were using "normal" passports when going for vacation or business outside Poland. They were still hard to get.

I was emigrating from Poland, my pasport was called "Consular Passport"

Later on (before I got USA citizenship and USA passport) when traveling outside USA I was using my Polish Consular Passport and document called "Re-entry Permit" to reenter USA.

I did not travel much but going to Brazil I had to get Brazilian visa.
No visa needs to get to Canada, just drivers' license.
Going to Poland I was using my Polish passport and Re-entry Permit on the way back.
After 5 years in USA I was able to get USA citizenship and passport.

/////////

..............

### Brent Patrick — 2022-10-17T03:55:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-316715>

It used to be 25 years but after 2014 the age is 35

**"For those who were granted an Age Pension and left Australia prior to 1 July 2014, the 25-year Australian working life residence will be applied. Should you return to Australia and then depart (after 1 July 2014), the new 35-year rule will be applied"**

[https://www.yourlifechoices.com.au/age-pension/faq/living-overseas-and-the-pension/](https://www.yourlifechoices.com.au/age-pension/faq/living-overseas-and-the-pension/)

### Jack Kruse — 2022-10-17T20:16:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-316728>

#BTC went from 69K to 18,700 and 66.1% of BTC has not moved in over 1 year. **_Let that sink in._** We have clearly bottomed and we have a very solid bottom based on this epic HODL amid a drop.

Bitcoin HODL Waves as one of the highest signal metrics with little worry of data overfitting to match bullish or bearish bias, as it’s not a complicated metric.
It visually lines up with the cyclical nature of Bitcoin. Right now we are likely on the cusp of step 4 on Oct 17th, 2022.

1.  Bitcoin’s supply is immutably verifiably scarce.

2.  Users recognize this, and they accumulate and hold coins.

3.  Floating supply becomes scarce.

4.  **Any (small) bullish catalyst occurs and the price moves up with few sellers.**

5.  Price moving up brings in more attention, education, and adoption.

6.  A parabolic bull run occurs as high bids start moving older coins.

7.  Price moves to an unsustainable level and crashes ~ 80%.

8.  Go back to step 1.

Using historical data, this metric is indicating that now is likely an ideal time to get exposure to BTC, and mining BTC enables you to capitalize on this twice. You own a mining rig asset that can appreciate with the price of BTC and it produces BTC cash flows that likely will increase while your energy costs remain fixed.

### caroline — 2022-11-19T21:16:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-317695>

update from ES.....

We finally have a new set of beautiful stairs to get up to our "penthouse" viewing platform.

We call this our stairway to heaven.....

Last night We were sitting watching the ocean and the sun go down and both of us made the comment - "there is no place in the world we would rather be"

We are so thrilled that we can have a swim and then sit up there in the morning and see the sun come up and watch the ocean while we enjoy a cup of Salvadoran coffee and homemade cranberry bread. I call that a trifecta!

We are pretty visible up there so we get lots of waves and Buenos Dias - como estas?

It is never too late to change direction in your life.

As long as you keep moving forward - even if you are scared

We reckon that a little fear and turmoil in your life is a good thing!

Humility and trust and love and faith and intuition go a long way on this journey.......

### caroline — 2022-12-03T16:20:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-318124>

RIP Christine McVie - Fleetwood Mac

One of our most favorite concerts. We paid big$$$ and sat close to the stage.

But then - Tony has been in love with Stevie Nicks his whole adult life....

### caroline — 2022-12-23T23:37:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-318506>

final update from ES for 2022.....

Wow what a year .... lots of ups and downs.

We have never lost our conviction that this move was the right decision for us.

We love our new home and our life here.

We are definitely a work in progress - on all fronts.

We have achieved so much this past year and I am so proud of both of us - especially Tony.

He is a legend ........he works so hard from early in the morning until dinner time with pool breaks.

Our pool is small but Tony is doing 30 laps 3 times a day .......It sounds rather funny but he is so proud of himself. He doesn't need to take a breathe to cross the pool!
He has a great stroke and kick now and it is paying dividends.

Just goes to prove that all things are possible and life is what we make of it.

Tony was told that he is at the end of his life and he should be back home in Australia where he belongs.

That says volumes about the person who told him that .........

We both wish everyone a happy Christmas with your loved ones .... and a remarkable 2023 as we all continue on this journey of self discovery.

### caroline — 2023-01-02T17:32:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-318638>

Have a healthy. happy and prosperous New Year everyone!

### notsoperfick — 2023-02-25T01:44:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-319732>

So in awe of your move Caroline, what a brave and amazing choice you both made! I fully understand why you decided to move away from Australia but I was totally astounded at where you moved to! I really hope all is going as well as you hoped and that you now feel comfortable and relaxed, it’s been fascinating reading about it all. Inspiring too.

### caroline — 2023-02-25T19:04:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-319747>

> [notsoperfick said:](/goto/post?id=319732)

I really hope all is going as well as you hoped and that you now feel comfortable and relaxed, it’s been fascinating reading about it all. Inspiring too.

I am not sure about brave and amazing.........but we both felt we could not stay in Oz.

We have been in El Salvador almost a year now - in fact on March 4th will be our first anniversary.

We are very proud of ourselves for doing what we had to - there was no option for us..

Absolutely no regrets.

### Jackie Nelson — 2023-02-25T22:54:06+0200

gradual U.S. collapse coming. It was so difficult to leave friends and now I also know that NZ is not a good place to be.
But I'm going to make the best of it and am happy, for now. The very best to you.

### caroline — 2023-02-27T23:01:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-319813>

Tony and I have been mulling over this past year.....

We have been following our instincts and acting accordingly. If we hadn't done that - things could have gone really badly.

I grew up thinking and being told that it was a bad/silly thing to follow your instincts.

It was here and from Jack that I learned to follow my instincts....thanks Jack.

### caroline — 2023-03-04T15:42:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-319864>

We arrived in El Salvador one year ago today.

We feel like we have accomplished so much - we have expanded our horizons and grown in so many ways.

We are both absolutely positive that there is no place we would rather be - right now.

We love our life here .....

We are so grateful for the salvadorans who help us daily adjust and adapt and learn.

We love the healthy simplicity of their lives and how capable they are in so many ways.

We thank them for their generosity and all the genuine kindness they show us every single day.

El Salvador has enriched our lives in so many unexpected ways.......

### caroline — 2023-03-04T16:07:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-319865>

We also need to thank Tom and Nancy for all their help in so many ways. We always had so many questions and they usually had answers.....except Nancy keeps buying up all the shredded coconut in El Salvador!
Thankyou to every one here who supports us and encourages us to keep moving forward.

What a journey we all are on!

Thanks Jack for all you do.

### caroline — 2023-03-05T18:48:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-319873>

Today is the beginning of our second year in El Salvador.........

We had a great party yesterday ......as Nancy said - a very eclectic mix of fun, positive, generous people.

One of our new friends/neighbours is totally all in on Jack - he can't get enuf of podcasts etc.

Other neighbors implement lots of Jack's stuff as well - red lites, sunrise and sunset etc. Thanks to Lisa.

We have found a great community here. The Salvadorans are kind, friendly and generous people.

Tony and Jose and Santos \[and me\] have done a great job of turning this house into a simple but wonderful home for us.
Of course we are still a work in progress! I will be sad to see the ladder/painting platform to be gone from the living room \[some day\]... ...Tom says it is an industrial style sofa back table.

We have the very best of outdoor living ...we are in awe every single day - we feel extra ordinarily lucky in every single way

I love Jack's latest blog ..........ALL THINGS EYES ....redox power

We have a water feature in our pool when we turn the pump/filtration/ozone system on - it is so relaxing to swim and or sit in the sun.

We are so contented here.

It almost feels wrong to be so contented .....we are so happy just to be here and enjoy our wonderful environment.

Of course there are days when the pump breaks down or we have no water or the electricity goes out ....all part of the charm and luck and living at the ocean in El Salvador.
I do take exception to birds flying thru our home and leaving poop......
Small trade off for massive returns.

Tony has a great all over tan .....

We can hear and see the ocean and there are lots of trees and bush and Tony is growing beautiful roses and we have planted lots of bougainvillea and all kinds of other stuff.
We are slowly learning what grows best here on the coast ...and how to keep the ants from eating it all.

The rainy season will start next month and everything will be so lush and green.

To be continued........

### caroline — 2023-03-11T21:02:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-319952>

We have a lizard that lives in our yard -one of the drains. He has become very friendly and walks around like he owns the place!

Yesterday Tony found him in a pot of Poinsettia - he has eaten and or mangled the whole plant and he was also eating the flowers off a beautiful hibiscus.......relationship over!

All this time we have only worried about the ants eating everything..........lesson learned.

Know your enemy.....don't be fooled by a placid exterior

### JanSz — 2023-03-11T21:12:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-319953>

Watch out for lizards on outside chairs when sun shines on them.
Do you have a little colorful frogs in your shower stall?

Tarantulas, snakes etc.

### Marko Pollo — 2023-03-11T23:41:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-319955>

I believe that if you can co exist with all the lifeforms that are in or belong in your environment, there will be far less bad ones overall. When you spray pests, you kill the predators also. The pests will bounce back first. This also kills lots of good stuff in the soil, which will then require fertilizer, which will attract more pests to your plants. Poinsettias will grow into giant hedges in the equatorial climate, not throw away Christmas plants. Same with lantanas and others! Having an overgrown jungle is way more sustainable than an orderly garden that attracts and is decimated by pests. Kill what you don't want with "la pantufla" or other mechanical methods. In my time in SA, lots of people warned of scorpions. I wore shoes rarely, got a couple of stings but they weren't the end of the world. The lizard probably ate lots of bugs too.

Poinsettias will grow into giant hedges in the equatorial climate, not throw away Christmas plants. Same with lantanas and others! Having an overgrown jungle is way more sustainable than an orderly garden that attracts and is decimated by pests. Kill what you don't want with "la pantufla" or other mechanical methods. In my time in SA, lots of people warned of scorpions. I wore shoes rarely, got a couple of stings but they weren't the end of the world. The lizard probably ate lots of bugs too.

thanks Marco .....We have had this beautiful poinsettia for many months and it has been so healthy and thriving. It was so sad to see it decimated this morning.

### caroline — 2023-03-13T20:41:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carolines-journal.5458/post-319990>

Tony and I are in awe of mother nature.

We are so tuned in now that we have an outdoor lifestyle.

We haven't had rain in months but now, all of a sudden, our bushes etc are forming new shoots and buds....even tho some of them are very straggly looking.

Two very large bushes that generally have huge pink flowers - look like a pile of sticks with new buds forming.

They must know that rain is coming.

All of a sudden all of our beauganvillia are perking up and lots of new shoots.
We have a gorgeous white mandevilla that is going crazy throwing out new climbers and also a baby red mandevilla that is climbing like crazy on some supports that Tony made for it.

It is truly amazing to watch mother nature in action.

They were 8 maybe 10 years old.
Last year four of them died.
Wonder why?
The four that died were relatively close to each other.
I still like them, and wondering if I should take cuttings from the one that survived?
Or should first figure out what might have happened and take corrective action first.
.........

### caroline — 2023-03-16T01:40:30+0200

I am loving the new series of blogs .........

We are so tuned into sunrises and sunsets and mother nature here.

It is fascinating to watch all the bushes and plants getting themselves ready for the rainy season coming up.

two big bushes were just a pile of twigs and now they have lots of new buds and leaves.

All the bouganvilia are starting to flower like crazy.

The mandevilla are climbing and flowering like they are possessed.

How do they know???

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

). I have read all blogs and comments and was wondering if below is right and you could help me clarify some things.
1\. You can eat a fourth meal if you are resetting AND have AF.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-14T18:41:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-adrenal-rx-during-leptin-reset.5459/post-70744>

3\. meals times were covered in the Leptin Rx blog, Ct 7 or 8 I believe. It may also be in how does the Leptin Rx work or Leptin Rx FAQ blogs

# Thread: Leverage your Health Insurance to make it work for you
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leverage-your-health-insurance-to-make-it-work-for-you.5460/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2013-01-14T10:55:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leverage-your-health-insurance-to-make-it-work-for-you.5460/post-70657>

So, many of you here on the board, know that Havoc has been taking speech therapy. Back in December I submitted the first set of claims to my health insurance company. I received notification at the beginning of Jan 13 - that those claims had been denied. So, I called up my health insurance and started asking questions. After a week of badgering - the insurance company actually told me verbatim what I needed to do to receive payment, based off Havoc's diagnosis codes (ICD codes).

According to my health insurance plan " The benefits for speech therapy state the plan will pay benefits for speech therapy only when the speech impediment or dysfunction results from injury, sickness, stroke, cancer, autism spectrum disorders or a congenital anomaly, or is needed following the placement of a cochlear implant."

They told me his medical file had 4 ICD codes, related to his ears and speech therapy (387.9, 382.9, 307.9, 784.59). The even pointed out which ICD code met the criteria above to receive speech therapy benefits, for Havoc. They also told me what language they needed in the letter from the provider to support the appeal of the current claims, and justification for all subsequent claims.

Here's what they told me to have the provider provide, on their letterhead: "Havoc 's speech impediment/dysfunciton, ICD 784.59. resulted from a congenital anomaly ICD 387.9. disformity in the ear which is pathological condition of the bony labyrinth of the ear, in which there is formation of spongy bone; may cause bony ankylosis of the stapes, resulting in conductive hearing loss; cochlear otosclerosis may also develop, resulting in sensorineural hearing loss."

So, now I'm waiting for the provider to write this letter and sign it. Once that happens I'll be appealing the initial claims submitted by providing this documentation, and all subsequent claims will have the same documentation submitted as well.

As a patient you have the right to know all of your diagnosis codes, and your health insurance company must provide them to you when you ask for them. So start asking people and use this as leverage to make your health insurance work for you. Additionally, always ask what's covered under your plan, and get it in writing. It used to be at the beginning of each new year the health insurance companies would send you a book of legalease that described in boring detail what was and was not covered by your plan. Now to "save paper" you can access it on line, if you know where to go. Be proactive - find out what's covered, and what isn't - so when you submit claims, and they get rejected - you know how to appeal each claim, and get the money you should be getting for services that are covered.

Additionally, you might want to consider submitting your medical claims yourself- instead of having your doctor do it for you. Why? you then have ownership of your health care. Take control of your health from all aspects of its management. Figure out how to make it work for you, not against you, this is just one component of Jack's health IPO http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-15-creating-your-health-ipo/

### SueC — 2013-01-14T12:13:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leverage-your-health-insurance-to-make-it-work-for-you.5460/post-70667>

Thanks for this! Another really helpful post.

Boohyah.

just received word that UHC will be covering Havoc's speech therapy. I have to say, having friends who are lawyers is a great thing. I had her review my last denial letter and the "plan" which is essentially a contract btwn me, my employer and UHC to cover services identified in the plan, and she was confident I had a Breach of Contract suit.  so I've been looking for contract lawyers here in the DC metro area. While looking for a contract lawyer - I also contacted HR and let them know what the issue was, and that I was seeking legal council for a breach of contact suit against UHC. I did this Tuesday.

well lo- and behold, late last night UHC agreed to pay for Havoc's speech therapy. Sometimes it pays to pull out the BIG GUNs. Guess UHC didn't want to play the legal route. Right now I'm waiting for confirmation that they will cover all remaining Speech Therapy sessions for 2012, and the sessions for 2013, once the cost has been applied to our deductible...

I'm going to blog about this so others can learn from this.

### Hope — 2013-03-22T02:47:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leverage-your-health-insurance-to-make-it-work-for-you.5460/post-81392>

Another thing is, and this is my experience with our HC company, is they are all too happy to do anything they can to delay paying anything, in our out of network. Many times, and I just recently had this with a 20th chiropractic visit that they claimed was the 21st and hence had to be denied....I got the runaround. First it was that,then that they used too many codes for one visit (when they had all been run the same way), all bologna. I finally got the chiropractor to contact them (actually, they offered) and verify with them and now they are finally reprocessing the claim...after two months. Though it's a small amount in this case, the principle still holds.

Personally, I don't like dealing with insurance at all. I wish there would be a thing where you put your own money into a fund, and then you can use it as you please - like for acupuncture, supplements, etc. and you pay for it from that fund, rather than being SO tied in to what insurance decides to cover or not. I wish all employees had access to something like that.

# Thread: Low Vitamin D Linked to Headache
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vitamin-d-linked-to-headache.5464/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2013-01-14T15:20:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vitamin-d-linked-to-headache.5464/post-70695>

.
Nonmigraine headache is associated with low levels of serum 25-hydroxyvitamin (OH) D, according to results from the sixth survey of the Norwegian TromsÃ¸ Study, TromsÃ¸ 6.

Marie Kjaergaard, MD, University Hospital of North Norway, TromsÃ¸, Norway, and colleagues found that the incidence of nonmigrainous headache was 20% higher in participants who had the lowest levels of serum 25(OH)D than in those with the highest levels.

In contrast, no association between serum 25(OH)D and migraine was identified in the same large population sample.

"\[T\]he VDR \[vitamin D receptor\] and 1-alfahydroxylase, which hydroxylates 25(OH)D to the active metabolite 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, are found in areas in the brain which are thought to be involved in the pathophysiology of headache and migraine, thereby indicating a role of vitamin D in these tissues," the authors write. "We found that low level ~of serum 25(OH)D were associated with non-migraine type of headache, and the association remained significant after adjustment for possible confounders."

The study is published in the November/December 2012 issue of Headache.

Few Case Reports

As investigators point out, only a few case reports have described the effect of vitamin D on headache, and no adequate intervention studies have been done.

Because others have reported a high prevalence of headache in association with high latitude, the authors felt these findings suggest a relationship between headache and vitamin D.

They subsequently explored this potential relationship in 11,614 participants in the sixth survey of the prospective TromsÃ¸ study.

The TromsÃ¸ Study began in 1974. This epidemiologic, prospective study of health problems and chronic diseases serves as a resource for the surveillance of disease risk factors. The study initially focused on cardiovascular disease but through the years also included several other chronic diseases and conditions, such as vitamin D status and chronic pain.

Participants were asked in a questionnaire whether they had experienced headache in the last year. If the answer was yes, they were asked to specify whether the headache was migraine or another type of headache, along with the average frequency, duration, intensity, and type.

A total of 725 participants indicated on the questionnaire that they suffered from migraines, although only about half of these fulfilled the group's criteria for migraine.

Another 3336 participants reported that they had another type of headache, but of these, 1051 fulfilled the group's migraine criteria.

The data were also stratified according to smoking status because the method of measurement used in the study was found after the study was completed, to overestimate serum 25(OH)D in smokers. "To adjust for this, we chose to divide the study population into current smokers and non-smokers," they write, with former smokers included in the nonsmoking group.

"In model 1 with adjustment for age, gender, BMI \[body mass index\] and chronic diseases, the non-smokers had a significantly higher OR \[odds ratio\] for non-migraine type of headache in the lowest quartile," investigators report. "This finding remained significant in model 2 when additional adjustment was done for the possible confounders physical exercise, alcohol consumption, and educational level."

In contrast, no association between headache and serum 25(OH)D was found in the current smoker group.

Risk for Headache Across Serum 25(OH)D Quartiles in Nonsmokers

Serum 25(OH)D

Migraine Headache (Nonsmokers)

Nonmigraine Headache (Nonsmokers)

Model 1

/>4th quartile

Reference

Reference

1st quartile

1.14 (0.76 - 1.71)

1.31 (1.14 - 1.49)

Model 2

4th quartile

Reference

Reference

1st quartile

1.03 (0.67 - 1.57)

1.20 (1.04 - 1.39)

As investigators note, it is important to consider confounding variables in a study such as this one because serum 25(OH)D levels reflect lifestyle and nutritional habits as well, both factors that can affect or be affected by headache.

Physical exercise, alcohol consumption, education level, and some chronic diseases also influence serum(OH)D levels.

The study was supported by the North Norway Regional Health Authority. The authors have disclosed no relevant financial relationships.

Headache. 2012;52:1499-1505. Abstract

Pam Harrison â€¢ Medscape Medical News Â© 2013 WebMD, LLC

http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1526-4610.2012.02250.x/abstract~

### Huck — 2013-01-14T15:57:19+0200

If I remember to, I will check vitamin d at the point the headache goes away again.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-07T04:20:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colicky-babies-may-have-wrong-gut-bacteria.5465/post-74563>

It \*is\* normal fo rmost babies to nurse several times a night from birth till age 3. That night nursing is key to keeping prolactin high enough to stave off fertility for mama. But the thing is, when mama's hormones are balanced (as mine were for the first four babes), mama & baby \*both\* sleep through all the nursings. Without even waking enough to remember it, they latch, and nurse for 30-90 minutes or more while both sleep.

When hormones aren't balanced (like with my fifth babe, and many of my friends' first & second & third... babes), it can be very difficult for the mother to sleep during nursing. Combine that with whackos preaching that it's unsafe to share sleep even if you're not drunk/drugged/obese/smoking, and yeah, most people can't sleep and nurse anymore. So ecological bf'ing no longer results in 3 years spacing naturally, as it has for millennia, and we "need" more hormones pumped into us to "fix" our fertility problem.

I don't see how people say it is bad...makes no sense for them to say that. And yet...these are the same parents that coddle their kids at way too old of ages. It screws up the kids. I think (and it's even in school systems too) that you should be as close to baby in the BEGINNING then the baby/toddler and then young child will end up being MORE independent to go off on their own, explore, go to school etc. For example, in Finland (only based on a documentary I saw so I am not trying to romanticize Finland) they were saying how children do not go to school until age 7. The child will stay at home and still 'learn' but that by the time they are 7 they WANT to go to school and are emotionally ready for such environment. \*To which they spend a majority of time outdoors anyway.

Thanks for your insights MamaG.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-07T05:11:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colicky-babies-may-have-wrong-gut-bacteria.5465/post-74583>

p.s. I am sorry that things went south for your 5th child. Strange how 4 pregnancies were fine and then all of a sudden bad health for the 5th.

You have made and are making a lot of progress and I am very happy for you. You are very inspirational. Keep on keeping. You are probably a wonderful mother by the sounds of it! That's a blessing for sure.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-07T05:33:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colicky-babies-may-have-wrong-gut-bacteria.5465/post-74585>

My 4th pregnancy sent my health downhill bad. But I didn't really see the effects until the next. Pregnancy is a heavy load on a woman's body, but the natural design is to feed women the super food before they get pregnant, while they are pregnant, and while they nurse, and allow the sleep-nursing and constant togetherness / frequent nursing to stave off fertility so that babies are spaced an average of 2.5-3.5 years apart (3 years is very standard), allowing each woman to have 5-8 children over the course of her fertile years without any degradation from the first to the last child, for either the mother or the children.

Weston Price showed this very clearly, and all research done on the matter since then has backed it up. There are tribes in the world still today showing it, and my own body did the same thing when I accidentally happened upon ecological breastfeeding while just following my instincts as a mother (and was lucky enough to have strong hormonal balance still).

so it's not at all surprising that the 5th went so differently (although her health is fantastic), given that I was still battling sugar addiction up till 2mo before I conceived, and had only a few years under my belt of good eating, after many generations of processed crap & increased detachment from the natural world.

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=74570)
>
> no its not.....unless your a modern encephalized human that thinks they are immune to evolutionary forces.

I don't know if you're referring to my statement that nursing through the night is normal for the first three years, but it absolutely is. It's indisputable by any science, and human beings (and primates) have been doing it for hundreds of thousands of years, probably longer.

Again, I said that BOTH mother and baby are sleeping solidly the whole time.

This IS evolutionary biology. It's how nature spaces children so that mothers' and babies' health don't go downhill with each subsequent birth. (The loss of this is why our society had such a desperate need for effective contraceptives to be invented in the 20th century, b/c babies were coming one after another, and with worse health with each one. Again, WAP's work, or heck, just read the early journals put out by Planned Parenthood founder Margaret Sanger to see how bad it got. Or you can look to "quiverfull" families today.)

> [Zorica Vuletic said:](/goto/post?id=74582)
>
> think (and it's even in school systems too) that you should be as close to baby in the BEGINNING then the baby/toddler and then young child will end up being MORE independent to go off on their own, explore, go to school etc. For example, in Finland (only based on a documentary I saw so I am not trying to romanticize Finland) they were saying how children do not go to school until age 7. The child will stay at home and still 'learn' but that by the time they are 7 they WANT to go to school and are emotionally ready for such environment.

It's so great that you are in touch with these instincts. There is a whole movement out there saying this, that ATTACHMENT early on leads to appropriate independence, in fact, greater independence, as they grow.

I'm a fan of "better late than early," too, as the title of a book that discusses the Finnish late schooling start shows. They have the best scholastic results in the world, last time I checked.

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-07T05:53:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colicky-babies-may-have-wrong-gut-bacteria.5465/post-74588>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=74585)
>
> I don't know if you're referring to my statement that nursing through the night is normal for the first three years, but it absolutely is. It's indisputable by any science, and human beings (and primates) have been doing it for hundreds of thousands of years, probably longer.
>
> Again, I said that BOTH mother and baby are sleeping solidly the whole time.
>
> This IS evolutionary biology. It's how nature spaces children so that mothers' and babies' health don't go downhill with each subsequent birth.
> It's so great that you are in touch with these instincts. There is a whole movement out there saying this, that ATTACHMENT early on leads to appropriate independence, in fact, greater independence, as they grow.

Amen.

Yes, this is the way women nursed all over the world forever. Day and night... frequent nursing, ongoing contact with mum, synchrony, calm HPA axis, etc. It grows great brains, it protects mothers, it provides contraception. (It's not only being pregnant too close together, but having to care for multiple children who are very young at the same time that overwhelms the mother's system.... though, I, too experienced the physiological slide downhill with my last pregnancy.)

Attachment theory is not a theory anymore. It is proven science, matched in rodent, primate and human studies since the 1940's and mirrored in an ongoing way by neuroscience. What I think secure attachment gives us that insecure does not, however, isn't just greater independence. It is greater interdependence... the ability to differentiate/be ourselves/stand on our own two feet while simultaneously forging strong connections with others and seeing our interconnectedness.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-07T16:03:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colicky-babies-may-have-wrong-gut-bacteria.5465/post-74635>

Totally agreed.

It took HARD WORK for "primitive" peoples to keep a woman's health strong for pregnancy after pregnancy. They succeeded.

We "forgot" how hard it is and just assume we'll come out of 1, 2, 5, 10 close-together pregnancies unscathed, even though we are 10 generations into nutritional (and other) causes of physical degradation, even though we are food (and electron) starved for 2-3 decades before bearing that child, even though we binge on pickles and ice cream while pregnant and laugh about it.

### lioness7 — 2013-02-07T19:26:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colicky-babies-may-have-wrong-gut-bacteria.5465/post-74660>

Attachment theory was "proven" by science......Yikes, since when does nurturing and common sense need a scientific study? I had baby #1 @ 25 - baby #7 @ 40...all were breastfed between 1-2 yrs. With the last couple, I noticed that they were not the best sleepers probably as a result of my worn out system. I nursed them all thru the night... they slept, i slept - none got suffocated, but I must confess, each one did land on the floor once (aghhhh, my husband used to call it "the initiation" each time we heard the thump).. I never had a "nursery" as they always slept in my room until they were ready for a real bed, or the next one was born. I had never heard of the term "attachment parenting", but I guess I did it instinctually. I never let them cry themselves to sleep...For the first year, I was at their beck and call. I vacuumed and cooked while nursing and when seated always had a book to read to pass the time for the longer feeds. I figured out how to crochet blankets and nurse at the same time with the first one.

Science is just only now recognizing the mind/gut connection. In previous generations, we didn't need to understand this connection. For years , mom's of colicky babies were told to simply switch formulas or avoid certain foods while nursing. My third one was the only colicky one for about two weeks which I know was as a result of my own beneficial bacteria issues which caused my only ever yeast infection during most of the pregnancy. I had only been on antibiotics 3/4 times in my life by 30 yrs, and was given a course during that pregnancy for a sinus infection, major mistake!...and so the yeastie beasties took advantage of a lowered immune system. Back then, I knew zero about probiotics, gut disbiosis, etc....I only knew about the importance of good, clean and natural food without the science to "back it up". I never used birth control (well, that's obvious lol) because I couldn't reconcile a little pill controlling something as complex as my reproductive ability...i.e. it must really mess with me. I questioned a lot about everything. I didn't always do my things right, looking back, but at least I tried my best.

Modern life has given us choices which have led to many mistakes for which we all pay. It is now crucial to continue to question everything and pass on the learning to others, especially our kids, so that future generations will not continue to become the victim of money driven life style, diet, medicine, science and technology.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-07T21:22:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colicky-babies-may-have-wrong-gut-bacteria.5465/post-74674>

Sadly, it needs science to prove it b/c there is a very vocal contingent out there still insisting that non-attachment parenting is best - not just "some of us can't do AP for XYZ reason and don't lay the guilt on us," but "cribs, countless devices to hold baby other than arms/sling, pacifiers, babysitters, etc., are BETTER for baby, SAFER, and HEALTHIR."

I never heard of AP or cosleeping or ecological breastfeeding or any of it, but just did what seemed right to me, even though I had never heard of anyone doing it in my life. I'd never even seen anyone breastfeed. I learned how to do it the same way I learned calculus - from a book! But when that babe was born, instinct took over and I don't know why, but no amount of programming from my friends & family & TV & magazines could override it. I'm very grateful.

> I questioned a lot about everything. I didn't always do my things right, looking back, but at least I tried my best.

Ain't that the truth!

I've been saying for years that every time we are faced with a new way to do something, we have to analyze it thoroughly. We can't just go "Oh, NEWER IS BETTER!" We have to look at the pros and the cons - the impact on health, wallet, environment, time. Usually the older way wins on the first three factors and it's a personal decision to weigh whether that's better for us or not. Too often, we just blindly do it b/c it's new, and that's what has gotten us in the mess we're in now.

### lioness7 — 2013-02-07T21:37:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colicky-babies-may-have-wrong-gut-bacteria.5465/post-74679>

Oh Mamagrok, you are so right, you just described it better than I did. Most of what people follow has also a huge convenience factor without the realization that the "convenience" will bite them in the butt one day.

### nonchalant — 2013-02-07T22:56:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colicky-babies-may-have-wrong-gut-bacteria.5465/post-74690>

I also remember being told to keep the baby out of our bed, but I was just too dang tired to do anything but wake up enough to start the baby breastfeeding, and then doze off immediately. Eventually the baby would wake and need burping, and I'd have to wake again, burp her/him, and crash asleep again.

### kathylu — 2013-02-07T23:35:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colicky-babies-may-have-wrong-gut-bacteria.5465/post-74695>

When I said "sleep through the night at 3 weeks" I meant a span of about 4 to 5 hours. The baby would wake once or twice in an 8 hour span to nurse. Worked for both of us! I also slept with baby by attaching his crib to my bed at mattress height. I could keep my hand on him and pull him to me easily. It also felt a little safer to me than having him on my mattress. It seems to have worked...he was a calm, happy baby who slept and ate exceptionally well. I feel so fortunate...I never had any problems with him...nothing like I have heard other mothers complain of. We did switch him to his own room at about 2 years old, but he made the transition in one day...he loved it! And of course he was welcome to sleep with us if he felt like he wanted to. He still does occasionally (he's 5 now).

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-08T01:11:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colicky-babies-may-have-wrong-gut-bacteria.5465/post-74729>

Yes, what kathylu's describing is very within the range of normal!

Mine have made the transition to their own room very easily, too, once I learned to let them make it on their own time. It's always somewhere between 2.5 & 3.5. We keep a crib mattress on our floor in case someone wants to come back and sleep near us (like for a stormy night or sickness), but no one ever has!

# Thread: Earthing with humidified air
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-with-humidified-air.5468/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2013-01-14T18:37:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-with-humidified-air.5468/post-70742>

Dr K has mentioned the importance of earthing - having contact with the ground as a source of electrons needed by the body. So I started looking at different options for grounding one's bed, etc. Then Dh said that if I'm after being electrically grounded, what we need is a proper humidifier in the house, and possibly an ionizer to improve it. And this is why static electricity is not a problem in the summer (sufficient humidity in the air), compared with the winter (inside anyways). So, I am wondering would air that has a reasonable humidity level be sufficient as an electron source for the body? It's like the WiFi of earthing devices... What I need is a voltmeter, my humidifier (which needs cleaning), and a little bit of experimenting. Thoughts?

### KiwiLauren — 2013-01-14T20:54:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-with-humidified-air.5468/post-70771>

So I started looking at different options for grounding one's bed, etc. Then Dh said that if I'm after being electrically grounded, what we need is a proper humidifier in the house, and possibly an ionizer to improve it. And this is why static electricity is not a problem in the summer (sufficient humidity in the air), compared with the winter (inside anyways). So, I am wondering would air that has a reasonable humidity level be sufficient as an electron source for the body? It's like the WiFi of earthing devices... What I need is a voltmeter, my humidifier (which needs cleaning), and a little bit of experimenting. Thoughts?

Interesting theory. I'd like to know the answer (I haven't a clue)... especially because there is no static electricity in my house in winter that I have noticed. But then, it is uninsulated and unheated. Maybe instead of a humidifier you can just turn off your heat and open your windows { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. ### Shijin13 — 2013-01-16T10:19:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/and-so-it-begins-welcome-to-levee-30-emf-1-is-now-live.5469/post-71026>

> **ashryn said**
>
> Here's a curious anecdote.. I don't wear a watch, because they stop when I am wearing them. If I take the watch off and leave it on the bench for a while, it starts working again. Put it back on... It stops.
> I am uncannily good at guessing what time it is, I figured it was because of practice, but maybe its because I don't have a little battery strapped to my pulse all day messing up my sense of world time.. What do you think?

I'm that way w/directions. when hiking outdoors, I'd rather trust my internal "compass" vs an actual compass. Turn me around and I can find N-S-E-W dead on. I did have a period where I lost this ability - it was when I was on depakote & topamax for migraines/bi-polar disorder. once I came off the meds - it came back. I also am pretty good at intuitively telling time as well...

I have a phillip stein watch that I love - but I haven't worn it in a while as I need to get the batteries replaced. the interesting thing is I can't wear it on my left wrist but I can wear it on my right. - Not sure their claim is valid or not - but the watch sure is pretty...

### diane — 2013-01-16T12:37:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/and-so-it-begins-welcome-to-levee-30-emf-1-is-now-live.5469/post-71041>

This blog was really thought provoking. Man - I'm almost scared to see what comes next to see what else I have to do! LOL!

I actually hate barefeet - have avoided it all my life. I hate sand on the beach. So must change attitude about all of this. That is definitely possible. I think that the barefeet thing is just because my feet are so sensitive that it hurts to walk on some surfaces.

Have been thinking a lot about my dogs and what my choices may be doing to them (which Dr. K confirmed in my journal - but very vaguely so looking for what's coming). Am also now thinking as to how I can incorporate this into my work with dogs. Will have to think more about this as well.

I, like Shinjin, work in an office building surrounded by lights, computers, servers, etc. A few years ago I noticed how horrible I felt when I was at work. I thought it might have been the air quality and then most recently the lighting (and lack of natural lights). Over a year ago, I had lost 20 lbs - and I worked in a 1 floor office building, near natural light, and went outside for a walk many lunch hours. Then I moved to a different building, on the 5th floor, no natural light. I gained back the 20 lbs in about 3 months. I have figured that something about the environment cause that increase in weight - and maybe it's a bunch of factors - but this latest blog I think adds another piece into that puzzle. So now I wonder if it's possible for me to be healthy while I work in this environment. I work from home about once a week - and notice the difference on the weeks that I don't.

I have also been feeling like I need a change from where I live - at least for the past few months. But now I'm feeling it even stronger, so will have to start figuring out my plan.

I can't wait to see where this all goes!

Here is a clue for you:

Why did Dinosaurs die and who was left? We know nothing about their molecular genomes, but what we do know, is that two other mammalian lines developed from them directly, the marsupials and the eutharian placentals. Humans are in the later class. How do we know placenta were heavily selected by a post K-T world? Well, the marsupials are only found in the southern hemisphere** with the few exceptions. This fits perfectly with how Factor X occurred 65 million years ago as well as I laid out in my Webinar. Moreover, the placental mammals were far more successful because they radiated over 2000 genera, to about only 140 genera in the marsupial clade.

Most of the surviving species left on the land of this planet is due to that formative moment in evolution. This is a big step in knowing who we really are and how we are built. Factor X is huge for any mammal that evolved after it.

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-01-18T21:21:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/and-so-it-begins-welcome-to-levee-30-emf-1-is-now-live.5469/post-71518>

Gauss is the most misunderstood term in magnetic therapy, greatly misused by some manufacturers who use manufacturerâ€™s gauss rating to indicate the strength of their products, when in truth the strength of a magnet depends on:

a) Strength of material (manufacturerâ€™s gauss rating)
b) Size and weight of the magnet

For example: The earthâ€™s magnetic field is 0.5 Gauss and a refrigerator magnet is 10 Gauss.

Considering only these numbers one would think that a refrigerator magnet is more powerful than the earth. This of course is incorrect, the earth, due to its immense size is immeasurably more powerful.

http://www.magnetictherapymagnets.com/gaussmeter.html
