### Eve — 2013-01-19T21:15:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-do-i-feel-better-with-higher-carb.5562/post-71719>

Why do I feel better with higher carb? I know carbs are "ok" but only in season. Because it's winter now, I know it's not optimal.
I remember reading on the forum that if you feel better on higher carb then it means something not good...but I don't remember why. Mitochondrial inefficiency?

Background: Leptin Reset since Jan 2011, leaky gut, lots of fish, very low carb...........I was craving carbs like mad today and had a banana, some honey and cashew butter and I feel SO much better. On a related note, I suspect I might be IR (as I'm definately LR) and am meeting with my doc on Tues to see the results of my FBG and fasting insulin and c-peptide.

### diane — 2013-01-22T11:46:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-do-i-feel-better-with-higher-carb.5562/post-72293>

In my experience, while I may feel better short term eating carbs, for me it fuels more and more need for them (which I'm guessing is because of mitochondria leakiness it's the short term energy and then you just need them to keep fuelling). But in the long term, I feel much worse. My own feeling and from what I read, is that perhaps there's something in my gut bacteria compelling me to eat carbs. And if I ignore it long enough, it stops. I believe that the carbs are doing more damage to me long term. I can't explain it scientifically, but from what I've seen happens to me. I imagine this comes from mitochondrial inefficiency and when I can fix this, (and perhaps gut issues?) I won't have the same issues with carbs in season.

In the Q&A (I think that's where it was) Dr K mentioned that it can take 18-24 months for our bodies to totally switch pathways and become effective fat-burners. I hadn't realised that before, but it makes sense and helps to understand the cravings (and why cravings signal LR).
> > Kiwi, was this detail revealed in the last Q&A ?
>
> Regarding Eve's question, I wonder, Kiwi, if it Is possible to totally resist the urge of eating these carbs and continue on, or is it necessary to give in to the carb craving in order to feel better afterwards? In other words, would it do more damage to simply not ingest the carbs--period?
> Well, I guess we wait until EMF3.

Yes, Dr Kruse talked about the 18-24 month (I hope I remember that number right) timeframe to switch to being a true fat burner. And that when you are not yet burning jet fuel (or Ferrari fuel as the case may be), 'slips' will set you back major.

So my thinking is that eating carbs out of season is definitely problematic... as would any sort of carb binge.

This is why, as I understand it, the leptin Rx has at its core the BAB and why the BAB is protein-heavy. Protein is a bridge between carbs and fat - it's easier to burn than fat for sugar burners but it keeps you away from carbs and keeps the cravings away. Obviously we need fat, too. And ideally we are looking to end up with a high fat, moderate protein diet. But we are transitioning the fuel source for our engines, to continue the metaphor, and this takes time.

I think any cravings = LR = not a fat burner = mitochondrial inefficiency. I also think this is why part of the Mitochondrial Rx (eg D-Ribose) are helping in getting our bodies to switch fuel sources more effectively. Food is one part of the equation, but as we are learning, being able to generate ATP is about way more than what we eat.

# Thread: EMF Protection - Dirty Power Dangers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-protection-dirty-power-dangers.5563/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2013-01-19T21:50:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-protection-dirty-power-dangers.5563/post-71725>

.
The power coming into your home is far more dangerous then RF from a cell phone tower. Rarely do you find anyone who is aware of this danger. It's been dubbed "dirty power" - the hertz levels coming into your home is what matters more than anything else.

â€œThe harmful biological effects of exposure to electrical pollution have been established in numerous studies conducted during the past 50 years.â€
Dr. Martin Graham, -Emeritus Professor of Computer Science and Electrical Engineering, University of California.

I was once like most people, I thought that electricity was homogenous. I thought that you paid for your electricity supply (240 volts or 110 volt depending on what part of the world you live in) and you got electricity. End of story. Well things are not quite as simple as that. Because there is also something called the quality of electricity. Thatâ€™s to say two houses in the same street can have the same 110 volts (or 240 volts) running through their internal wiring system, from the same source but the quality of electricity can vary by multiples of 10 or even 100. This quality of electricity can be measured by using something called a Graham Stetzer meter or GS meter. Using a GS meter and installing a collection of GS filters, a GS Filter system, is the means by which your electricity can be rendered of good quality and safe

GS filter system is a means of transforming dirty electricity back into good quality power. It filters out electromagnetic pollution in the range 4 to 150 Khz (that which Russian research has shown to be the most dangerous) carried in your power supply.

The human body is an electrical being. All the cells, tissues and organs in your body communicate with each other via small electrical currents. Dirty electricity perturbs these communications which makes the body vulnerable to disease.

Studies show that there is a biological impact to electromagnetic pollution. Cell phone towers and cell phones produce one form of this pollution, but dirty electricity produces another. Just as deadly.

Sam Milham in his book â€œDirty Electricity : Electrification and the diseases of civilization â€. defines dirty electricity as high frequency voltage transients on electrical wiring.

Dr Milham analyzed death records from electrified and rural areas in the early part of the 20th-century and compared them with statistics from areas where there was no electrification. He discovered a correlation between electrification and such diseases as cancer, diabetes, heart disease, suicide, neurodegenerative disease. He found that where electricity had been introduced the disease rates were much higher.

Interestingly he also found that the Amish community, who donâ€™t have electricity, have lower rates of heart disease, cancer, diabetes etc

Dirty electricity is much more prevalent today because of the electrical appliances that weâ€™ve got so fond of and depend on. In the Southern California middle school that I studied, there was a 21% increase per year (in dirty electricity) if you worked in the school. If you worked in a room that had a reading that was too high for the Graham/Stetzer meter to measure; that was above 2000 units, a teacherâ€™s risk to cancer was increased by 26% per year, in a decade it is 26% times 10 higher, thatâ€™s a 260% increase. If you compare the teachers in this school to the California population they had 10 times the risk of melanoma and 11 times the risk of uterus cancer.

\[video=youtube\_share;vbebpRvwd8k\]http://youtu.be/vbebpRvwd8k\[/video\]

http://www.stetzerizer-us.com/meter.html

# Thread: Anyone with Phase Delay Sleep Disorder?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-phase-delay-sleep-disorder.5575/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lee — 2013-01-20T18:08:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-phase-delay-sleep-disorder.5575/post-71901>

Hi All -

Does anyone else have this? It's been with me since I was a baby and as a child I regularly didn't get to sleep until 3-4 am. My mother was also this way but took sleeping pills to get around it, which I never have. I've fought it for school and jobs, but was miserable living in normal people's schedules. Now I work for a place that allows us to keep our own hours, and there are others on the team who are night owls as well. We do our best thinking and work from 10 to 2 am, some earlier and some later until 5 or 6am.

It's estimated to affect 10% of the population pretty uniformly across the globe. I speculate that we were the folks who stayed up and tended the fire while the rest of the tribe slept. On Pingelap Island, there was a natural disaster in the last couple hundred years that reduced the population down to under a hundred and the night owl gene became very prominent and large percent of the island's population are night owls, who are also colorblind with excellent night vision. (My husband is this way.) He's in amazing shape. Eats Paleo. CT's and sleeps around 4 am.

When I did my cortisol saliva test, I didn't get enough sleep the night before, had low morning cortisol, and when I filled my last tube at midnight I was dozing and had my husband wake me up so I wouldn't miss it. That last cortisol was high and the graph ran pretty much straight across on the lower third of the scale. If I manage to touch sleep it's initially very fragile, and anything that wakes me when I'm falling asleep means that I have a terrible time getting back to sleep.

Does anyone know how the circadian regulation of the Leptin / epi-Paleo Rx works with people like us? When I try to fit my circadian rhythms with others' I have a lot of trouble, and I've tried my whole life. It's only since I've had work that allows me to keep my own hours that I've felt much better. I keep the house dark starting at sundown and get my face into the sun first thing when I wake up. I take sublingual gaba around 10. I also take melatonin and try very hard to get to sleep by 1am and wake up around 9-10. This is the best I've been able to do and stlll feel comfortable.

I'm pretty sure there's a reason for this gene, and it makes sense that it would travel with the good night vision gene that my husband and the Pingelap islanders have.

I'd love to hear people's thoughts on this.

### fitness@home — 2013-01-20T20:28:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-phase-delay-sleep-disorder.5575/post-71927>

I don't have much to add, but wanted to say I found your post very interesting. My 30 year old son has always been a night owl (as was his father).

For myself, I find it had to make it past 8:30 p.m. My body pretty much shuts down and is ready for rest.

Am interested in reading feedback from others...

### ashryn — 2013-01-21T05:44:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-phase-delay-sleep-disorder.5575/post-71982>

I too do my best thinking in the middle of the night, and left to my own devices I'd sleep till 1pm each day and stay up till 3am. Over many years I have struggled to train my brain to sleep at night, so I don't have it as bad as you, but I do feel that by forcing myself to be up and about during the morning and asleep early, I am missing out on the creative spark and sense of engagement that comes to me when I am up in the middle of the night.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-21T22:20:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-phase-delay-sleep-disorder.5575/post-72229>

i hit this one in the Sleep Rx.......no idea when I am going to launch it. Kinda dropping nuclear bombs right now in January.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-21T22:21:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-phase-delay-sleep-disorder.5575/post-72231>

And all my stuff works huge with it.......you need electrons before you go to bed......and as soon as you rise. Photon therapy is going to cure it......and I personally think ECT makes a ton of sense but I know no one will do it for this indacation

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-01-22T10:49:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-phase-delay-sleep-disorder.5575/post-72284>

For the poor people, I hope hugging trees is at least good enough in morning and before bed. That is what I'm doing now instead of relying on morning sun. Because in winter it doesn't rise until too late or doesn't show. Hands and face against a tree----instaed of ECG for ppl that can't afford weird stuff you talk about...

That's my story and I'm sticking to it. Please let me know if it's at least good enough. I don't like if there is a feeling of in accessibility.

O what about rocks versus trees? Does one have more electrons than the other..? Just curious.

Also I want plants and rocks in my room now. (But I wanted this for when I moved ----and never did...b/c I allocated my money for food...) But maybe I can take a small ceder tree or something and transplant it instead.

I see what you mean about the ECG though and probably setting it to 7.83 Hz. I can definitely see that benefit. I would like to use this technique for my brother who definitely has some hypothalamic damage and as a result MANY hormonal and transmitter imbalances. I wonder how a person can even live like that....amazing on the one hand what the human body can withstand...but wow...it's sad b/c he actually feels like he is an 'old man' (age 28). He literally says O it feels weird to be old. Um....? Djezus. Too young for that.

Also I feel like there is always a constant parallel between me and my brother except divergent path....like it was so easy for me to have been like him...but I refused to not help myself. Also I was 'in tune' with nature (not talking about diet b/c as kids we just ate normal but still home cooked meals so this included dairy and grains but veggies and meat too and VERY little processed foods). I climbed trees, he played video games (but he played hockey sometimes...just not as much outdoor as I.

Before age 7 was better (for him) because again we were (both) outside so often. Lucky to have a child hood spent outdoors. But I climbed trees and got in the mud more. We both swam in the lake luckily (oooooo nostalgia time). I think par for par though, I spent more time outdoors than him (as a kid). Although both compared to MANY kids back then and espeically to now a days: We both spent much much more time outside (the way kids SHOULD be).

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-01-22T10:50:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-phase-delay-sleep-disorder.5575/post-72286>

And btw, now that he is 'calm and stable' for past months. What does he do....all day watch tv in blacked out room. Maybe eats maybe not. Sleeps a ton...but sleeping better at night...but maybe he watches tv (on low) during night too. Gets up many times during the night to go to the bathroom or to walk outside or something.

Shitty life I say.

### lee — 2013-01-22T14:04:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-phase-delay-sleep-disorder.5575/post-72314>

@Jack - ECT? What is ECT? - Is there a glossary? I'm liking your electron bombs...if you can fix this so I'm feeling the optimal mind during the day, I'll give it an honest shot.

@Ashrin - G'day! Yes. The creativity is key. I'm fascinated by what happens in those wee hours when I'm so productive and wonderfully cerebral. Whenever I've forced myself into another sleep cycle, I lose something very significant and definitely enjoy life less, even though I'm awake when everyone else is. Even if I get 8 hours, I don't feel rested.

I'm way curious about what causes this effect. Is it a magical combination of neurotransmitters that only happens with late nighters? Is it higher cortisol combined with something else?

I've been to sleep clinics. I've worked all kinds of different shifts, but there's only been one schedule where I feel genuinely alive that feels natural. In the last 10 years, I've been lucky to be able to work my own hours, with others who are doing the same. The topic always comes up at some point. It's such a huge relief to be able to function where my mind gets to feel optimal as well.

# Thread: What did you eat today?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-did-you-eat-today.5581/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Reset 2013
**Posts:** 33
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TheKid — 2013-01-21T04:23:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-did-you-eat-today.5581/post-71977>

I kicked off the Challenge today with 2 cans of sardines, baby clams and veggies. Oh, and some nice extra virgin olive oil. I am thinking that success for me is really going to be highly correlated with convenience in the morning. I've failed in the past, I think, because of my inabilitity to eat early enough. And I was not eating early enough, because I did not have food ready and on hand immediately upon waking.

So my strategy is to go with leftovers or canned seafood. And I've stocked the cabinets with sardines, clams, oysters, salmon, mussels, etc.

Good luck to everyone!

### caroline — 2013-01-21T06:44:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-did-you-eat-today.5581/post-71989>

### TheKid — 2013-01-23T08:55:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-did-you-eat-today.5581/post-72489>

I've continued with sardines in the morning. But I think I need to put some other stuff on rotation, so maybe it's time to add the oysters, clams and mussels.

Lunch continues to be very large salads with some grilled chicken chunks.

Dinner is usually something very hearty: mostly lamb and some sort of steamed veggie.

One of the strategies for dietary change management that Tim Ferriss uses in the 4-Hour Body is to eat the same meals over and over again. This is my approach in order to avoid missteps that have plagued me in the past. My main misstep was waking up with nothing ready eat which often meant too long of a delay on the BAB. It would also lead to delying eating sometimes until I got to work. We have a breakfast spot next door where I would do large amounts of eggs. But then the smell of coffee would draw me in. And what's coffee without cream? Since I'm supposed to really have neither (Hashis, adrenal fatigue), those trips to that spot would throw me off.

Easy to do with seafood, very difficult to do with eggs and bacon!! (mmmm bacon)

Lunch was 1 lb of mussels and clams and chocolate bark. and dinner will be osso bucco in the slow cooker { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Better get to the kitchen and prep today's lunch. Breakfast will be easy...leftovers.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-10T23:29:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-did-you-eat-today.5581/post-75285>

Okay, I admit to falling off the wagon yesterday. Hadn't gone grocery shopping yet and took the easy way out for meals. Didn't help things that we were on the go a lot.

Spent today doing preps for some meals this week. Baked some Paleo meatballs and bacon this morning. Also made an Indian chicken dish & cauliflower rice this afternoon. The freezer is restocked with seafood. Wild Alaskan salmon was on sale and it is already grilled and ready for breakfast.

Back on the wagon.

Today's lunch and dinner were the exact same - lobster and scallops topped with cremini mushrooms and avocado. Pretty boring, but tasty too

The way I understand it, the net positive charge is due to the atmosphere itself. The higher above ground level you get the more positively charged the atmosphere is. When you are constantly insulated from the ground you would be exposed to net positively charged particles from the air which would bind with any free electrons you have floating around in your meat suit. I'm not sure about the quantity of positive charge you are exposed to when insulated vs. the quantity of free electrons you get with food and water, but it seems it would favor the positive side.

### bigknitwit — 2013-01-22T20:12:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inflammation-has-a-net-positive-charge-where-is-the.5586/post-72396>

I just can't make sense of explanations that involve talking about postiive and negative charges, and our need to neutralize (ground ourselves), when I can't make sense of where the unwanted charge was generated (which would tell me where the equal negative charge is left). Am I making sense here?
>
> The way I understand it, the net positive charge is due to the atmosphere itself. The higher above ground level you get the more positively charged the atmosphere is. When you are constantly insulated from the ground you would be exposed to net positively charged particles from the air which would bind with any free electrons you have floating around in your meat suit. I'm not sure about the quantity of positive charge you are exposed to when insulated vs. the quantity of free electrons you get with food and water, but it seems it would favor the positive side.

Yes, of course! I spent the better part of the weekend trying to come to grips with the fact that the earth's exterior is negatively charged, balanced by a positive atmosphere/ionisphere. I'm feeling pretty comfortable with this now. So, when I am up in the sky (disconnected), I am losing my charge because I am at a lesser potential than the atmosphere in which I reside. If my feet were properly grounded, this would be a non-issue, since I would be refilled accordingly (and held at ground potential). But I'm generally not grounded, and if I live in an apartment, I'm much further from being grounded, and my potential difference relative to the earth is so much more, I lose electrons much faster. So I suffer worse losses. To make up for some of these losses, I can use CoQ10 (do birds use unreal amounts of this stuff??), but in general, I am born to be grounded, not to fly. So this is why some people report reduction of inflammatory symptoms when they ground themselves regularly...

Now how do those electrons find their way to the right location to put out the inflammation fire? Laws of attraction, right? They travel pretty quickly, and water is pretty conductive, so would water ions transport them right to my inflammed wrist? I have not (yet) experienced this. I quickly rigged together a grounded cable attached to a resistor, which alligator clips to my clothes (waistband) while I'm sleeping. When I eat something I shouldn't, I notice the result within 10 hours by a light pain in my dominant wrist, along with weight gain (fluid retention). This just happened the other day (although I'm not sure what the food culprit was, even now), but the grounding cable made no difference to the course of events (the weight gain, wrist pain, time duration of the reaction, etc). I will need to think on this some more, and consider a larger grounded area than just the aliigator clip (although sleeping on aluminum foil proved to be a failure lol).

### Jason Coates — 2017-05-17T16:18:11+0300

### Jason Coates — 2017-05-17T16:18:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inflammation-has-a-net-positive-charge-where-is-the.5586/post-214199>

What is inflammation any way? It is the excess of protons and/or loss of electrons at your surfaces. The surfaces being the eye, gut, and skin. So inflammation sounds like the final common pathway of nrf2 and NFkappa beta..........all tied back to calcium efflux. Calcium is the concrete for your cell membrane lipid bilayer. It keeps them welded together to act as a native EMF antenna. All paths lead to low plasma volume and loss of RBC mass decreasing the zeta of the plasma and dehydration and loss of divalent atoms in cell membranes and plasma at once.......That volume and chemistry change is sensed by the 7 areas in the brain with no BBB........most important one and first one affected is PVN........but when you get Candida that bad........area postrema is really off because the third surface interaction with light is off......namely the gut. What connects that surface with area postrema? Vagus nerve. Stress breaks down all barrier or surface chemistry and this alters the interaction and direction of energy flows from the environment to the cells below........surface chemistry dictates what happens in the deep layers in our tissues.

You can't out supplement, out hack, out exercise, or out eat a bad environment.........that is the story of the ubiquitin series. It's nature's axiomatic rules of engagement. So until that time you the clinician must realize that the patient is trading wellness for time............

There is no set point for metabolism; too many patients and clinicians however, believe otherwise and this is why diet is their first tool. Metabolism is on a sliding scale of natural selection built into ubiquitin. Ubiquitin is the interaction of light with nitrogen in proteins.. Its boundaries are limited only by the how we account for and assimilate and contain the electrons from our environment. Set points are creations of concrete reductive minds. That is not the work of evolutionary biology nor is it following nature’s law’s.

If you believe the nonsense about food and metabolism ask yourself this: At what age do you hit your set point? The concept is vacuous. It confuses negative and positive feedback loops with an attempt to attain a steady-state outcome. I spoke about this in webinars and in the ubiquitin series at length. Loss of negative feedback is deadly in a far from equilibrium state, why? In that scenario when control is lost both prey and predator die. For example, when a patient gets Candida in their gut from any stressor.......you've lost predator and prey in your microbiome, the biome gets smaller in number and species, because your surface chemistry is destroyed.

In a non-linear system, there may be no equilibria or there may be many. Either one is possible. That makes it fundamentally quantum. Possibilities exists until the waveform is collapsed by the environment you allow. I think the 900 pound plus people we have in the USA and OZ have gone into a zone of the non-linear dynamics where the negative feedback loops in our cells ceases to limit fat mass as a protective mechanism of a toxic environment to our surface chemistry.

### Jason Coates — 2018-05-31T13:38:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inflammation-has-a-net-positive-charge-where-is-the.5586/post-242440>

Leptin is King of the Hill \_\_\_ Branched-chain amino acids in metabolic signalling and insulin resistance
Too much protein? No protein pills at night, Major Tom?
Branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs) are important nutrient signals that have direct and indirect effects. Frequently, BCAAs have been reported to mediate antiobesity effects, especially in rodent models. However, circulating levels of BCAAs tend to be increased in individuals with obesity and are associated with worse metabolic health and future insulin resistance or type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM). A hypothesized mechanism linking increased levels of BCAAs and T2DM involves leucine-mediated activation of the mammalian target of rapamycin complex 1 (mTORC1), which results in uncoupling of insulin signalling at an early stage. A BCAA dysmetabolism model proposes that the accumulation of mitotoxic metabolites (and not BCAAs _per se_) promotes β-cell mitochondrial dysfunction, stress signalling and apoptosis associated with T2DM. Alternatively, insulin resistance might promote aminoacidaemia by increasing the protein degradation that insulin normally suppresses, and/or by eliciting an impairment of efficient BCAA oxidative metabolism in some tissues. Whether and how impaired BCAA metabolism might occur in obesity is discussed in this Review. Research on the role of individual and model-dependent differences in BCAA metabolism is needed, as several genes (_BCKDHA_, _PPM1K_, _IVD_ and _KLF15_) have been designated as candidate genes for obesity and/or T2DM in humans, and distinct phenotypes of tissue-specific branched chain ketoacid dehydrogenase complex activity have been detected in animal models of obesity and T2DM. [https://www.nature.com/articles/nrendo.2014.171](https://www.nature.com/articles/nrendo.2014.171)

# Thread: Fasting???
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting.5590/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2013-01-22T11:40:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting.5590/post-72292>

Just a word of caution.

I just had my dr appt and doctor recommended I have a vitamin a lab as I hadn't had one for several years. The nurse came in and drew the blood. Today I was notified results were in and they were 97(38-98). This seemed high compared to previous labs, so I did a little research.

Here's what I learned on the LEF site:

> Fasting for a minimum of 8 hours is required. No foods containing vitamin A should be ingested in the previous 48 hours. Take all medications as prescribed. Do not take supplements the morning of the blood draw.

I wasn't fasting, had broccoli the previous night, and I had taken my supplements including one with vitamin c. Neither the doc or nurse asked if I was fasting.

So I guess the best thing would be to ask the doctor if fasting is required before they draw the blood.

# Thread: 350V from the top of your head to ground
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/350v-from-the-top-of-your-head-to-ground.5593/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2013-01-22T15:20:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/350v-from-the-top-of-your-head-to-ground.5593/post-72335>

"If you are standing outside on a clear sunny day, wearing shoes or standing on an insulating surface like a wood or plastic/vinyl floor, there is an electrical charge of some 350 volts between the Earth and the top of your head if you are 5 feet 9 inches (1.75m) tall." (EMF 1)

V=I\*R
I = V/R
Wet skin resistance is 100 ohms. Dry skin is 100Kish ohms

If I go outside wearing my rubber boots, and then touch the ground with my finger, I would expect Ohm's law solved for current to look something like this:

I = 350/100 = 3.5A

Okay, now I wouldn't be 5 9 if I were bending down, so 350 V is not entirely accurate, and the 100 ohms could indeed be much more if my skin wasn't drenched. Both would reduce the resulting current. (350/100 000 = 0.0035A = 3.5mA)
However, doesn't this seem like a potential for a massive electrical discharge = electrical shock upon touching the ground in some circumstances? Since I have never seen, or heard of this happening, something else is not adding up. I am truly trying to understand here. In reality, if the ground were wet and the person was wet, than there would be moisture in the air (ions), which would significantly discharge the person before they touch the ground. What else am I missing?

### Shijin13 — 2013-01-22T15:47:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/350v-from-the-top-of-your-head-to-ground.5593/post-72339>

I've seen and felt it happen on a flight line... its one of the reasons you always ground yourself to something metal before getting on board if walking - I also always ground my self before fueling up... I look crazy but static electricity can cause serious shit around gas

> However, doesn't this seem like a potential for a massive electrical discharge = electrical shock upon touching the ground in some circumstances? Since I have never seen, or heard of this happening, something else is not adding up. I am truly trying to understand here. What else am I missing?

Your missing the critical element.......the Schumann resonace........the pulse of the Earth is missing from your numbers............you are not alone there, ironically.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-01-22T19:32:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/350v-from-the-top-of-your-head-to-ground.5593/post-72387>

How can I create Schumann resonance? Does it spew from trees? lol

### diane — 2013-01-22T19:38:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/350v-from-the-top-of-your-head-to-ground.5593/post-72389>

It's not something we create - it comes from the earth. Watch the video in the EMF-1 blog and it explains what it is. And it's an amazing (and scary) video! But I'm so glad I watched it!

### bigknitwit — 2013-01-23T09:39:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/350v-from-the-top-of-your-head-to-ground.5593/post-72492>

> **Jack Kruse said
>
>
> > bigknitwit said
>
> **
>
> > "If you are standing outside on a clear sunny day, wearing shoes or standing on an insulating surface like a wood or plastic/vinyl floor, there is an electrical charge of some 350 volts between the Earth and the top of your head if you are 5 feet 9 inches (1.75m) tall." (EMF 1)
> > V=I\*R
> > I = V/R
> > Wet skin resistance is 100 ohms. Dry skin is 100Kish ohms
> > If I go outside wearing my rubber boots, and then touch the ground with my finger, I would expect Ohm's law solved for current to look something like this:
> > I = 350/100 = 3.5A
> > Okay, now I wouldn't be 5 9 if I were bending down, so 350 V is not entirely accurate, and the 100 ohms could indeed be much more if my skin wasn't drenched. Both would reduce the resulting current. (350/100 000 = 0.0035A = 3.5mA)
> > However, doesn't this seem like a potential for a massive electrical discharge = electrical shock upon touching the ground in some circumstances? Since I have never seen, or heard of this happening, something else is not adding up. I am truly trying to understand here. In reality, if the ground were wet and the person was wet, than there would be moisture in the air (ions), which would significantly discharge the person before they touch the ground. What else am I missing?
>
> Your missing the critical element.......the Schumann resonace........the pulse of the Earth is missing from your numbers............you are not alone there, ironically.

My mind is spinning with all of this... I have to think about it some more. Between the earth and the ionisphere is basically... a capacitor? That would change the impedance part of the equation. I need to look into this more (I graduated 13 years ago, so while I get the lingo, it's been a good long while since I put it to serious practice). And now I need to think about the EMF part of this whole thing...

### kahunalamaku@gmail.com — 2013-01-26T21:38:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-sure-tree-hugging-is-so-smart-after-all.5594/post-72751>

### ruby — 2013-01-27T03:37:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-sure-tree-hugging-is-so-smart-after-all.5594/post-72821>

Hi Kahunamalaku,

Polish folk medicine is heavily focused on tree energy healing. The star of the lot is birch - it's famous for great conductivity and accelerated grounding effects. Birch juice, when drawn from the trunk first thing in the spring when the tree "awakes" is thought to have great nourisment value. They sell it bottled in supermarkets but fresh is best, obviously  I guess in the past when food was scarce, it was the earliest source of plant carbs in the spring before everything else started growing.
The info I cited comes from here: [http://www.uzytkowanielasu.zafriko.pl/kat/energie\_drzew](http://www.uzytkowanielasu.zafriko.pl/kat/energie_drzew) -- try google translate, maybe it will make some sense  Otherwise google "tree energy" or "birch healing" or "birch grounding". I am sure something will come up

# Thread: Missed almost 2 weeks
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/missed-almost-2-weeks.5598/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cowboy — 2013-01-22T19:25:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/missed-almost-2-weeks.5598/post-72383>

Ended up very busy with work projects and buisness projects, and a bad cold and just could not find the afternoon time for CT, so I missed almost 10 days. Got back in today and after the first 30 seconds at 51 degrees I though I wasn't going to be able to stand it for more than a minute. Got some shivers and some shakes but slowly it started to feel ok, not good but ok. Started reading and the next thing I knew 20 minutes had passed. Guess I am back in the saddle again. We also had below zero temps (not just wind chill) during that missed period, and -15 really makes you wonder about CT. Also last Friday i ended up overstressing a should due to some ridiculous heavy akward lifting. Was out of town and away from my tank so used ice packs on it. Finally after three days I regained pretty good range of motion. After the 20 minutes in the tank, it feels really like I had no injury.

# Thread: Leucine Deficiency
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leucine-deficiency.5602/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glen PDQ — 2013-01-23T00:25:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leucine-deficiency.5602/post-72460>

Interesting that leucine deficiency mimics calorie restriction, leads to decreased abdominal fat, and stimulates BAT and UCP1 (uncoupling protein 1).
http://www.landesbioscience.com/journals/adipocyte/article/18951/?show\_full\_text=true

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-23T10:55:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leucine-deficiency.5602/post-72505>

makes total sense........when you realize that leucine is found mostly in foods that should up in fall and winter.........another reason why PHD is dead wrong.

### NittDan — 2013-01-23T11:53:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leucine-deficiency.5602/post-72512>

Looked up leucine rich foods and saw things like egg whites, soy protein isolate, game meats, and lots of fish species.

Are we saying that one should be deficient in leucine to lose fat, and therefore should stay away from these foods???

### Glen PDQ — 2013-01-23T13:21:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leucine-deficiency.5602/post-72521>

Nobody wants to be deficient in a nutrient. Leucine is also important for protein metabolism. Several other independant factors increase BAT directly or indirectly: shorter days, cold, something called BMP7 (bone morphogenetic protein 7) which increases THERMOGENIN aka UCP1, and norepinephrine. Thermogenin is activated by fatty acids and inhibited by purine nucleotides. ATP is one of the purine nucleotides.

### NittDan — 2013-01-24T10:43:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leucine-deficiency.5602/post-72652>

Making my head hurt, Glen... { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. # Thread: Electrical Engineer need apply,  what conducts
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/electrical-engineer-need-apply-what-conducts.5607/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2013-01-23T10:25:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/electrical-engineer-need-apply-what-conducts.5607/post-72496>

1\. Do all "skins" conduct for the purpose of "earthing" for lack of a better word. Bearskins? unknown leather shoes with shearling lining? What else.

Lets figure out some McGyver stuff. I did a ton of research about this time last year, as I built an "optimal" backyard space. Despite all objections I did not vapor barrier or week barrier anything and made sure all was on actual earth. But there are areas that have a sand base, and areas that are concrete blocks and some large boulders we sit on.

Is there way to "check" this stuff?

Next outdoor project - which is on slate for this summer is getting the "soaking tub" built. Since metal is so dangerous at the low temps, I'm trying to decide on something that would also conduct through the saline water. . .it will be a salt water cold soak tub lowered in the dirt. . . any suggestions/ideas/warning?

### endless — 2013-01-23T12:29:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/electrical-engineer-need-apply-what-conducts.5607/post-72515>

Clay? Not sure how you'd do that, but it should work.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-01-23T13:47:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/electrical-engineer-need-apply-what-conducts.5607/post-72525>

I like the clay idea just from an aesthetic standpoint. Like the clay pots to cook in. I think that would look great in your backyard with all the natural rock you have! Now the question is does it make sense from a grounding standpoint as well as the financial outlay.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2013-01-23T14:06:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/electrical-engineer-need-apply-what-conducts.5607/post-72533>

Oh yea! Clay sounds awesome. I have something to googles on now. . .

### bigknitwit — 2013-01-23T14:47:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/electrical-engineer-need-apply-what-conducts.5607/post-72545>

I think pretty much all skins will conduct because they are water permeable. However, when they are dry, I think they're pretty resistive. Basically the conductivity will vary with the degree to which they are wet.

### DarleenMB — 2013-01-23T15:18:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/electrical-engineer-need-apply-what-conducts.5607/post-72550>

have you ever handled wet clay? It's slippery as an eel. Wyoming, in addition to sitting on a lot of coal, has lots of clay. There are locations all around the area where the hillsides have gotten saturated from rains and then slid down on themselves taking trees and shrubs along with them. Everything stays pretty much intact but the land looks folded in on itself.

Years ago they redid Big Goose Road and one day I was coming back into town and it had been raining. There's a spot that curves as the road goes downhill. It's all clay. When I hit that spot my car just started sliding ... I could feel the traction disappear. A truck was trying to come up the hill (and it's really not that much a of grade) and the wheels were spinning like mad as the truck slid backwards.

So no, I don't think I'd be using clay unless you want a slip 'n slide.

I'm curious about our house though so I'm glad you brought this up. We're on a concrete block foundation with a 4' crawl space over good old dirt. I'm wondering if we're getting any kind of earthing effect or if that 4' air space just negates it.

### DarleenMB — 2013-01-23T15:20:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/electrical-engineer-need-apply-what-conducts.5607/post-72551>

Forgot to address the 2nd part of your question. Concrete topped with basalt tiles?

### ashryn — 2013-01-23T17:10:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/electrical-engineer-need-apply-what-conducts.5607/post-72563>

Get a giant terracotta planter, plug the hole, seal it to stop the water soaking into it and making it moldy?

### Birdy — 2013-01-23T22:28:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/electrical-engineer-need-apply-what-conducts.5607/post-72602>

If you are concerned about conductivity you can use a metal tub and cover the bottom with a mat. This way, you will be in direct contact with the water, which will be in contact with the metal, which should give a good contact with soil underneath if it is moist. http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrical\_resistivity\_and\_conductivity

# Thread: Mitochondrial Rx - results?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-rx-results.5609/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lyndra — 2013-01-23T12:48:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-rx-results.5609/post-72518>

Anybody else following the mito rx and seeing results?

I've been taking Q10 in the ubiquinone form for many years to try to stave off migraines (with some limited success). About 3 weeks ago I switched to ubiquinol (the reduced form) but cut back my dosage to 100 mg/day, thinking it was more bioavailable and that would be good enough. After learning more about mito health, just a week ago I decided to start ramping up. For the last two days, I've taken 600 mg/day. The last 2 nights I have had reasonably good sleep, staying asleep for 4-5 hours before waking and not waking with an urgent need to pee. Compared to my usual of waking every 2 hours, this is a huge improvement. I'm also taking d-ribose and r-lipoic acid but quit l-carnitine because it was giving me headaches.

Time to order a boatload of ubiquinol to see how much better I can get.

### Glen PDQ — 2013-01-23T12:57:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-rx-results.5609/post-72520>

I get NOW brand 100 mg Ubiquinol (60 softgels) for just under $28. It uses the Kaneka delivery system. Anyone found this same reduced form any cheaper?

I had my eye on the Carnosine for several months and bought it a couple days ago. Maybe it will be synergistic with my Carnitine.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-01-23T13:41:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-rx-results.5609/post-72523>

Lyndra I wonder if you could add the l-carnitine back in once you replenished your ubiquinol. I'm thinking l-carnitine is not good for you by itself unless you have enough ubiquinol to balance it. Just a though because at this point I have no idea if I am right or not.

### Lyndra — 2013-01-23T14:24:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-rx-results.5609/post-72538>

> **Patty Cakes said**
>
> Lyndra I wonder if you could add the l-carnitine back in once you replenished your ubiquinol. I'm thinking l-carnitine is not good for you by itself unless you have enough ubiquinol to balance it. Just a though because at this point I have no idea if I am right or not.

That's what I am hoping to do once I heal a bit more. Dr. K said my mito need help as evidenced by getting migraines when I take various amino acids, including carnitine. While I would love to dive in and do everything and get better RIGHT NOW, past experience has shown me I need to ease into it so I can gauge the effect one supplement at a time.

### bigknitwit — 2013-01-23T14:43:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-rx-results.5609/post-72544>

This idea is interesting to me! I started ribose, then carnitine 2 weeks later, then ubiquinol 2 weeks later again. In the first few days of carnitine (at high dose), I could start to feel a positive (almost calming/more mental resilience) difference. I was excited! By the time I was 10ish days into the carnitine, I was starting to get some rather dramatic set-backs thyroid-wise (feeling very hypoT). I took out the carnitine, and the symptoms resolved. How/why does ubiquinol need to be replenished before carnitine is added in?

It could just be that some bug has knocked me, from the same time I started to d ribose (Healthy Origins brand) 3 days ago.

# Thread: It's catching on .... among the middle aged :-)

### ruby — 2013-01-23T20:25:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-catching-on-among-the-middle-aged.5611/post-72582>

Theyâ€™ve been meeting every weekend morning like this for the past two years -- never less than two, sometimes as many as 15, and their numbers are growing.
Ranging in age from 44 to 57, they head off shortly after 9 a.m., jogging down the southern side of the hill.This is no ordinary running group. In their professional lives they work for banks, hedge funds and private-equity firms. The 25-minute run is just a preamble. Soon they strip to swimming shorts, gloves and swimming caps, not wetsuits -- and dive into a pond, which on this day is at barely 2 degrees Celsius, or 36 degrees Fahrenheit. It can dip well below that.
http://www.bloomberg.com/news/2013-01-21/bankers-fund-managers-strip-for-sub-zero-swims-in-london.html

### Inger — 2013-01-24T04:37:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-catching-on-among-the-middle-aged.5611/post-72634>

### endless — 2013-01-31T18:06:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-links.5614/post-73463>

I ordered an earthing rod and wrist strap from Earthing canada, and yesterday it arrived. Here is a quick review for anyone interested.

I have been experiencing acuter back and neck pain for the last several weeks. I have sciatic pain in both legs, nerve pain from my neck in both arms and hands, and wake up (frequently!) with numb fingers. I've been using chiropractic, massage, acupuncture, stretching, CT, etc.... Last night I slept with the earthing strap on, and slept all night with no pain or numbness. I woke up feeling far less inflammation. I can feel the actual locus of my problem, in my neck, instead of my entire spine throbbing.

I'm happy, and convinced. For $50 I just took a huge leap back towards wellness when I've been shelling out $$$ for treatments that haven't been working.

### Rachelle — 2013-01-31T19:49:07+0200

### fitness@home — 2013-01-31T20:32:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-links.5614/post-73504>

Thanks for the review. We need more reviews on this thread!

### nonchalant — 2013-02-12T22:54:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-links.5614/post-75602>

I just thought about our doorbell. Ours went out a couple years ago, and instead of fixing the wiring in the attic, we just installed one of those wireless bells that plug into an outlet. Wonder how much that puts out. Darn conveniences!

### shilohman — 2013-02-13T02:42:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-links.5614/post-75640>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=73504)
>
> Krista,
>
> Thanks for the review. We need more reviews on this thread!
>
> By now, many people here have purchased grounding items. I have not...waiting for more feedback

At first I was rather skeptical, but thought I would give it a try. Here is what I did:
I bought a grounding strap from the local electronic supply store ($8) and then I took a
an unused computer power cable. I cut the two flat prongs off the end that goes into the outlet
and left only the round ground prong. Next I cut the end off that goes to the computer. I peeled back
the insulation so that the green, white and black wires were exposed. I stripped the insulation off of the
green ground wire. I put the grounding strap on my wrist and used the alligator clip at the other end to
clamp onto the bare ground wire. So for less than $10 I figured I would give it a try.

My first night I plugged the ground into the wall outlet, clipped the alligator clip to the bare ground wire and put the grounding strap around my wrist. Personally, I expected nothing to happen, it just seemed too weird to be worth doing anything. But I was wrong, I woke up that morning having slept much deeper and restful than I had for many months. The second night I figured my first night might just be the placebo effect, so I did it again and now my sleep is better than it ever has been, much deep and better rested. I wouldn't dream of sleeping without being grounded. I also ground all day while working on the computer. So that's my experience with grounding and I would strongly recommend it to anyone to try.

### caroline — 2013-02-13T02:50:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-links.5614/post-75643>

Great to be getting people's feedback - thanks

### nonchalant — 2013-02-13T03:33:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-links.5614/post-75646>

I got a small grounding pad on clearance from lessEMF.com, along with a plug that is essentially like what shiloman did. I tested it with a regular AC/DC/current electrical meter, first detecting the AC voltage coming from my body, trying to get to ground. I was picking up this voltage just by sitting in the room. I saw that the voltage increased when I got closer to the computer, and skyrocketed if I put the laptop actually on my lap. Then I touched the grounding pad (with my hand or foot) and watched that voltage quickly drop to zero. I also tested various outlets in my home, and found that some of them are not properly grounded.

I don't have an EMF meter, but measuring AC voltage coming from my body while in the proximity of various devices seems to be an acceptable substitute. I have used the grounding mat, plugged into a grounded outlet, for a couple weeks now, on and off during the day, and at night. I actually don't feel different. But I don't like the idea of all that AC voltage without a route to ground.

One curious note, however, I've noticed that during the night DH, who is not grounded, seems to be touching me more, while he is asleep. He will have a forearm touching my shoulder, then he will move in his sleep, and then his foot touches me. Then he rolls over, and now it's just a finger, barely touching me as he snores away. Maybe it's my imagination, but I don't remember this constant connection previously.

I'd like to try grounding soon with a dedicated ground rod, at night.

### endless — 2013-02-13T03:50:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-links.5614/post-75650>

> Endless, in order not to have this pain/numbness you would need to wear this earthing strap all the time? I am assuming no real curing effect takes place and that you would have to wear it all the time.

Well, I do not have the problem all the time. As I said, it is an acute problem that has lasted for several weeks and during the time I am grounded, I feel a reduction in pain and numbness. I think it is reducing the inflammation in my back and neck. I assume that reducing the inflammation is going to help resolve the issue so I think it's worth it.

I like the ground rod idea better than the outlet, personally.

### shilohman — 2013-02-13T04:12:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-links.5614/post-75653>

If you are putting in an individual grounding rod and you have multiple bedrooms that you want grounded you might consider paying an electrican to put in an isolated ground to your house wiring. You would just have him put the outlets for the bedrooms on the isolated ground. An electrican would install an earth ground and then run that to the house breaker box. Then the grounds for the bedroom outlets are moved to the isolated ground bar. Probably cost $300-400 to get done. Just a thought.

### hellojtm — 2013-01-29T23:00:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-2-is-now-live-nuclear-is-the-word.5615/post-73226>

Some major problems I have with this EMF/E=MC^2 stuff.

1\. E=MC2, you can't change C2, it's a constant. It's the speed of light.
2\. You can't reverse the equation, the only changes you can make are Energy or Mass.
3\. You don't have to be grounded for induction to occur. I.e. if you were standing on 40" of the world's best insulator, wearing Chuck Taylor's, the electromagnetic inductance on your body would be just as strong as if you were standing naked in a pool of battery acid at the beach.
4\. Cold doesn't slow down electrons, in fact cold reduces resistance, in standard materials, and thus makes current travel faster (even though the electrons don't move very far/fast as some commenters on the blog post mentioned, and as I got schooled on in a job interview "trick question"). In semi-conductors (silicon) heat can speed up current. I.e. there is a temperature balance that needs to be struck across different materials to optimize current. Our warm-blooded bodies solved this problem (creating heat) way back before KT and it's another reason we overtook the reptiles. Our nervous system can operate optimally (warm) even in very cold temps, unlike a cold-blooded animal, like a frog.
5\. The cold that we do in CT isn't meant to make us so cold that life slows down, its meant to trigger the epigenetic expression that promotes autophagy, telomerase, and cellular signaling, hormone sensitivity, etc... (per Jack)

I'm just trying to build up from a set of basic premises to get to where Jack is going with all of this. I believe there is something to grounding but the inclusion of E=MC^2 to me is superfluous. E=MC^2 seems most appropriately applied to humans as such: you have mass, therefore you have potential energy. The amount of energy that is contained in your body is equal to your MASS multiplied times the speed of light (c) squared. So, I don't see Jack's big picture. What is it? It's sounding like disconnected paradigms now.

Support:
[http://3.bp.blogspot.com/-Ek9nAAfnHFc/TrPJ\_ZAcJ\_I/AAAAAAAAADY/LPx4dcp8bSQ/s1600/earth-magfield.jpg](http://3.bp.blogspot.com/-Ek9nAAfnHFc/TrPJ_ZAcJ_I/AAAAAAAAADY/LPx4dcp8bSQ/s1600/earth-magfield.jpg)
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrical\_resistivity\_and\_conductivity#Temperature\_dependence](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrical_resistivity_and_conductivity#Temperature_dependence)
On Dexterity and It's Development, page 85:
"First, the gigantic size of the reptiles in the Jurassic and Cretaceous periods was by itself disastrous.
As they say in physics, each reaction has it's temperature coefficient; that is, it proceeds faster at high
temperatures. This principle is also true for the phenomena in the neuromuscular system. It is well
known and has been accurately measured that the speed of transmission of the electrochemical excitatory
signal, the neural impulse, differs greatly between cold-blooded and warm-blooded animals. A wave of
excitation travels along frog nerves at a speed of 8-10 m/s \[meters per second\], whereas it runs along cat
or human nerves at a speed of 100-200m/s. Moreover, it has been established that this speed depends only
on the type of the nerve (motor nerves are the fastest) and on body temperature but not on size of the body."

### hellojtm — 2013-01-30T04:25:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-2-is-now-live-nuclear-is-the-word.5615/post-73289>

Thanks Anna. I think that's orthogonal to the CT idea, because I recall (I think) Jack saying that you aren't trying to reduce your core temp for CT. But, it is interesting nonetheless and actually more relevant to the topic at hand than icing is. But, it's still quite confusing to me to somehow tie the speed of light (which is practically a constant, if not theoretically) to electron "flow" in the cells.

The conventional wisdom is that if you reduced the speed of light then your mass would increase, so that E is constant. If E is going up, and the speed of light were reduced the cells/object would have to gain \*even more mass\*. If, however you wanted E to go down, then you'd have to lose mass, or reduce the speed of light. Or, simply lose E. Energy and Mass are correlated. But it's kind of like the theory behind calories in calories out isn't it? _It's just tautological at some point._ We can't really control the E or the M or the C2. Just for funsies I'll re-read EMF1 and EMF2, to see if there is some plausible way in which the cells are changing the speed of light in any way other than passively (the same way water hitting your pool changes the speed of light).

C is a constant in a medium. So if it crosses mediums it's still a constant. From wikipedia:

"The speed of light in vacuum, commonly denoted c, is a universal physical constant important in many areas of physics."

It goes on to explain how the speed of light is not the same through various media, such as a glass. But you wouldn't pretend that the wine glass gained mass or gained or lost energy because of this. Even though it may somehow be related to the mass of the material in some physics-nerd way.

jesse

But it's kind of like the theory behind calories in calories out isn't it? _It's just tautological at some point._ We can't really control the E or the M or the C2. Just for funsies I'll re-read EMF1 and EMF2, to see if there is some plausible way in which the cells are changing the speed of light in any way other than passively (the same way water hitting your pool changes the speed of light).
>
> C is a constant in a medium. So if it crosses mediums it's still a constant. From wikipedia:
>
> "The speed of light in vacuum, commonly denoted c, is a universal physical constant important in many areas of physics."
>
> It goes on to explain how the speed of light is not the same through various media, such as a glass. But you wouldn't pretend that the wine glass gained mass or gained or lost energy because of this. Even though it may somehow be related to the mass of the material in some physics-nerd way.
>
> jesse

mass would increase.......you keep thinking that means weight........Time is a function of mass in Einstein's physics, so as it increase for biology it means your life span increases..........

### hellojtm — 2013-01-30T21:42:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-2-is-now-live-nuclear-is-the-word.5615/post-73380>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=73303)
>
> mass would increase.......you keep thinking that means weight........Time is a function of mass in Einstein's physics, so as it increase for biology it means your life span increases..........

I don't think mass means weight. Mass is atomic mass. The sum mass of the parts of the molecules in the substance. Including protons, electrons, god particles, etc...

But with an increase in mass comes an increase in the effects of gravity so... essentially you would also appear to weigh more, on earth. But, I don't think that's really the issue here.

Where time comes into e=mc2 is in the speed of light. Speed depends on time. You can't measure speed without a unit of time. If anything, the equation does a great job of showing the "speed limit of the known universe", which of course will probably be debunked at some point anyway  (at least I hope so, progress!). In that if you try to go too fast you'll eventually become so massive that you'll cause a black hole. That's some speeding ticket.

So let's take the time is a function of mass argument. You have these cells, your bodies cells, living on a clock, your circadian rhythm. Essentially a 24 hour rhythm (ignoring any cycles longer than a day). So you futz with the mass of your cells, which \*in your theory\*, slows the rate at which they operate. But each 24 hours they still need to get done whatever was required of them. For example, taking up nutrients, would I want that to slow down too? Producing hormones, anti-oxidants, etc... do I want to slow that down? It seems to me if you slow down the cells but keep the same circadian "schedule" you'll miss "deadlines". Does mucking with the speed of light really increase your life span? Or just decrease the rate at which an individual cell achieves it's life span, which doesn't mean that the organism is going to die any sooner, all factors considered?

jesse

# Thread: Get your Gut Tested for a $99 donation! Only 10 days left to do so.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-your-gut-tested-for-a-99-donation-only-10-days-left-to-do-so.5616/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2013-01-24T09:25:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-your-gut-tested-for-a-99-donation-only-10-days-left-to-do-so.5616/post-72642>

http://www.indiegogo.com/americangut
American Gut - what's in your gut?

World's LARGEST open-source, community driven effort to characterize the microbial diversity of the American public. Does diet matter? Only one way to find out.

The Human Microbiome Project and other microbiome projects worldwide have laid an important foundation for understanding the trillions of microbes that inhabits each of our bodies. However, opportunities for the public to get involved in such research has been limited. Now, American Gut gives you an opportunity to participate and to compare the microbes in your gut to those in the guts of thousands of other people in the US and elsewhere. American Gut is a project built on open-source, open-access principles. Our data are for the good of understanding and will be shared both with participants and with other scientists. Our experience has been that our best ideas and work come when we involve people in as many steps of our work as possible, be they scientists, educators, roofers, ultra-marathon runners or corporate leaders. Everyone has something to offer, whether their sample, their hypotheses, their analyses or their dog (yes, their dog, we will get back to that). The more we can understand the complex microbial ecosystems on which we depend, the more everyone will benefit.

See how your microbiome compares to others and learn how your diet & lifestyle may shape your gut microbiome
Our team of 30 scientists has published over 800 papers
Our team has sequenced thousands of samples from all over the world
We start mailing out the PERKs (kits) in February (note that this is a "Flexible Funding" campaign and anyone who joins will be able to participate, even if we do not reach our funding goal. Giddy up folks!)

# Thread: How much is too much (oysters)?

**Posts:** 5

### duceswild557@hotmail.com — 2013-01-24T10:10:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-is-too-much-oysters.5617/post-72648>

Is 1-2 pounds of oyster meat going to do any damage? I've been eating this for a week now and my libido has gone up something ridiculous, also my workouts have improved and I find my anxiety has gone down while concentration has gone up. When I find something good though I tend to overdo it sometimes. Is 1-2 pounds of oysters for a 6'3" 200 lb young male who's active too much? And by too much all I really care about is will I develop some sort of toxicity doing this long term?

### Shijin13 — 2013-01-24T10:44:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-is-too-much-oysters.5617/post-72653>

I'm guessing Jack would say no amount of oysters is too much. they're natures perfect food for your body and brain. eat up... If you can clip that much down go for it! the more the better...

### duceswild557@hotmail.com — 2013-01-24T11:02:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-is-too-much-oysters.5617/post-72654>

If that's true then I am very excited, lol, I don't want to stop eating these at this amount. Since introducing them I've noticed significant changes and I know that jack considers them the #1 food in bg6. They hook my bowels up, and though I don't think he says this anywhere, I seem to get sleepier sooner than I used to so I'm not sure if they're helping my circadian rhythm or what. But I think he's right because I've experimented with a lot of foods over the years and if I had to pick one single food item, to have for the rest of my life, oysters would be it, hands down.

### Inger — 2013-01-24T11:47:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-is-too-much-oysters.5617/post-72658>

Duces, I feel the same way about oysters you do.. if I had the money I would eat only these..huh! They are energizing in such a nice way and so juicy and tasty,,,
I get me 12-13 pieces every friday and I enjoy them immensely. I am sure Gretchen is right about that you can not eat too much. Very kind of you to share your experiences!

### duceswild557@hotmail.com — 2013-01-24T12:39:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-is-too-much-oysters.5617/post-72666>

Inger, definitely, I like many of Jack's ideas and, considering he is a neurosurgeon, if he writes a blog and says that the best food in the world is oysters. "1. The base of the Pyramid is shellfish (oysters) other than crustaceans: Provides the most nutrient density of any food source for optimal functioning of the human brain."-bg6 then I'm gonna take his advice, lol. I tried taking his advice a few months ago by getting fresh oysters from the market near me and eating them raw, but I got seriously I'll after eating a maybe questionable set of oysters. I recovered fully in under a week so maybe it wasn't that bad but then I remembered jack saying that the worst seafood is better than the best meat so I bought some bumblebee canned oysters for 2bucks per 8oz. And while this isn't as good as the fresh thing, it's more affordable, (I'm a college student so that's Important) and I don't have to worry about catching something if my supplies is questionable since they are actually cooked. But basically everything I've come across for oysters looks positive, they have dopamine, they probably increase sex hormones more than any other single food and they have o3s, selenium and b12 in huge amounts along with iron and iodine. My stools have improved, not that they were bad before and I feel sharper, though I concede that could be placebo, even though I don't think it is. So yea, I just figured that of anywhere I could post on e net about eating oysters, I'd probably get the most constructive feedback here, and that's true so far.

1\. Manmade EMF blocks the flow of the Earth's electrons into us
I am struggling to understand this, but it's critical. It tells me Jack says grounding in the presence of unnatural EMF fields will not result in electrons flowing into us. For current to not flow with a direct connection, it means there is no potential difference between us, or that conductivity has been lost. Or? I am missing something here. Also, the Earth itself has it's own E-M field, that clearly does not disrupt electron flow since we're designed for it. What is different about man-made EMFs versus the Earth's natural EMF (or the interaction of the two), that creates this effect? Also, what frequencies are of particular concern? AM and FM radio have been around for quite some time and they are both an E-M field at particular frequencies.

2\. The body requires an uninterrupted flow of electrons to fuel it's mitochondria. We don't eat 100% of the time (although start to explain why I always want to be eating). Therefore, we are designed to "fill in the gaps" with electrons from the ground itself. When I am not eating, and haven't eaten, my cells are still being delivered "food" though, aren't they? The "food" in this case is from my fat stores. This would provide plenty of electrons, a huge supply (as shown by people who fast over several week's time). So while I'm not putting food in my mouth, I still have plenty of reserves. This directly competes (in my mind) with Jack's saying about our need for ground electricity. There must be an answer for this that I am not readily grasping.

Jack - I saw your responses yesterday morning, and spent the day pondering, reading more, etc. I hope they reappear, along with everyone else's responses. I have been thinking about this point further, and I think the issue is that I need to think of the body as having a resistance and a capacitance. So in essense, between the earth and me is a capacitor, and between the top of my head and the ionisphere is another capacitor. At low frequencies (Schumann), a capacitor is basically an open circuit, and there is no leakage current. I would only be losing electrons through my resistivity, which would make for a slow leak into the atmosphere. However, at higher frequencies, a capacitor now starts conducting AC current, so I would see an AC current being coupled onto my body thanks to the various AC currents from all the EMF we have introduced. However, these would be ecceedingly low currents. If my feet are making a good conductive connection to the earth, then I lose the extra capacitor, since my body is now just an extension of the ground. I think this is a the real take home, now that I type it. Even an "optimal" body was never meant to provide all it's electron needs from it's own stores. 6 years ago I picked up and moved from Houston TX to Colorado so I could live more according to nature and my human programming. That doesn't mean I've not stayed up late watching tv, the more I learn and the more I scaffold behaviors - editing and changing based on what I've learned ALONG WITH WHAT WORKS IN MY LIFE. For me, doing something unpleasant for a long time with no results = stress and anxiety. SO, I abandon some things, try others. . two steps forward, one step back.

after my son was born, I started making changes to become the person I wanted to be and the mother my son deserved. Am I optimal? Nope, still 30 lbds overweight - but when you put it in perspective I've come a Loooonnnnggggg way baby. I weigh less now than I did in 6th grade.

Schumann resonance has to do with the impulse response of lightning (emits all frequencies). They all hit the ionisphere which acts as a filter for most of them, except the ELF band. 2\. With the signals all tuned (light/dark/Schumann), it's time to hit system reset = cycloset (or potent mix of herbs?). My clock reboots, senses the correct incoming signals, and hopefully, I can remove the reset signal and continue on my own.

3\. Now that I can tell time, the other protocols will start to have a dramatic impact on me.

>
> Inflammation, below 1

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-10T14:44:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-2-q-and-a-hopefully.5618/post-75202>

Cavemam It's where I've been coming up lately too!! You articulate it best of course. If I compare to a year ago then I should not be complaining at all.  I am definitely much stronger and happier NOW than before. Such a good point that a lot of people get confused (myself included) over personal successes and trying to reach for perfection (even if perfection is not stated in absolute terms).

Now we've got essentially the debate started by Barry kicking around here again.

It's good to experiment with yourself and even try to test 'what you can get away with' sometimes since sometimes in a backwards route it might end up causing you less stress and therefore less inflammation. No one here is going to back to a SAD diet anyway...so having a few more sweet potatoes here and there and in winter, well maybe the situation calls for it---like having dinner with friends and/or family....and add a glass of wine while you're at it.

At different times throughout the year I felt so paralysed sometimes to have simple fun with friends and also with a guy friend. I had given up a few events (and even as recent as New Years) b/c I was so afraid of not being able to 'do what is right and then I will fall apart even more etc etc etc'. It just got ridiculous and I might have to feel a regret when I'm old in my 90s for choosing to not go to my friends' cottage for Canada Day....

Right...lots of little changes which are accumulative that will eventually magnify a best life possible for YOU.

p.s. Cavemam you are rock solid and I just have to give you :clap hands:! You chose your life and you engineered it, but it did not happen over night, nor was it (what it seems you say) all unpleasentness either. If something seemed too unpleasant for too long then you abandoned it, since it would only stress you out instead of progressing you.

So Big Knit Twit: Keep on keepin' and start to learn your personal wiggle room and don't stress about the 'small stuff'. Maybe you do a little bit of CT in a manner that suits you, and maybe you do a little bit of less EMFs here and there etc. Be proud of yourself!!
